title: The Record Format & Batches
abstract: A deep, source-grounded reference on Kafka's on-disk and on-wire byte format. It dissects the v2 DefaultRecordBatch header field-by-field (with byte offsets and the attributes bitfield), the varint/zig-zag DefaultRecord body, the legacy v0/v1 message format and why v2 superseded it, control records (transaction markers and KRaft quorum events), compression codecs and CRC-32C, and the MemoryRecordsBuilder/FileLogInputStream machinery that writes and reads these bytes. It also shows how idempotence/transactions are carried in the batch header (PID/epoch/baseSequence).
sections: Where it lives in the code
Data structure: the v2 record batch (on disk / on wire)
Data structure: the v2 inner record
Legacy formats (v0 / v1) and why v2 exists
keyFacts: The v2 record batch (DefaultRecordBatch) has a fixed 61-byte header (RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD = RECORDS_OFFSET = 61); fields in order: baseOffset(int64,off0), batchLength(int32,off8), partitionLeaderEpoch(int32,off12), magic(int8,off16), crc(uint32,off17), attributes(int16,off21), lastOffsetDelta(int32,off23), baseTimestamp(int64,off27), maxTimestamp(int64,off35), producerId(int64,off43), producerEpoch(int16,off51), baseSequence(int32,off53), recordsCount(int32,off57), then records (DefaultRecordBatch.java:104-131).

The batch CRC is CRC-32C (Castagnoli, via java.util.zip.CRC32C) computed over bytes from ATTRIBUTES_OFFSET(21) to end; it deliberately excludes partitionLeaderEpoch (bytes 12-15) so the broker can stamp the leader epoch without recomputing the checksum (DefaultRecordBatch.java:67-71, 386-388, 399-401; Crc32C.java:41-59).

The v2 attributes byte packs: bits0-2 compression codec (mask 0x07), bit3 timestamp type (0x08; 0=CREATE_TIME,1=LOG_APPEND_TIME), bit4 transactional (0x10), bit5 control batch (0x20), bit6 delete-horizon (0x40); bits7-15 unused. Only the low byte is used (DefaultRecordBatch.java:99-101,133-137,403-406).

Idempotence and transactions live in the v2 batch HEADER: producerId, producerEpoch, baseSequence are header fields; non-idempotent batches use sentinels NO_PRODUCER_ID=-1, NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH=-1, NO_SEQUENCE=-1 (RecordBatch.java:57-59; DefaultRecordBatch.java:122-127).

The v2 inner record (DefaultRecord) is delta-encoded: body length(varint), attributes(int8, always 0), timestampDelta(varlong), offsetDelta(varint), keyLen(varint,-1=null), key, valueLen(varint,-1=null/tombstone), value, headerCount(varint), then [headerKeyLen,key,headerValueLen,value]*. Max fixed overhead MAX_RECORD_OVERHEAD=21 bytes (DefaultRecord.java:36-72,168-223).

Kafka varints are base-128 with zig-zag mapping zz(n)=(n<<1)^(n>>31) for int (1-5 bytes) and (n<<1)^(n>>63) for long (1-9 bytes); null fields encode as varint -1 (one byte). Encoded width is computed via a leading-zeros intrinsic, not a loop (ByteUtils.java:474-487,498-516,557-603).

CompressionType is an enum with a 2-bit-storable id: 0 none, 1 gzip(JDK Deflater), 2 snappy, 3 lz4 (level 1-17, default 9), 4 zstd (level -131072..22, default 3). Codec classes load lazily to avoid pulling native libs unless used. Decompression buffer sizes: gzip/zstd 16KB, lz4 2KB intermediate; LZ4 uses the frame format with magic 0x184D2204 (CompressionType.java:29-130; GzipCompression.java:80-83; ZstdCompression.java:105-109; Lz4Compression.java:66-72; Lz4BlockOutputStream.java:39).

In v2 the entire records region (after the 61-byte header) is compressed as one stream while the header stays plaintext, so offsets/PID/CRC are readable without decompressing (DefaultRecordBatch.java:64-66,273-277).

Control batches set attribute bit 5; their records use a typed key (Version:int16,Type:int16). ControlRecordType ids: 0 ABORT, 1 COMMIT, 2 LEADER_CHANGE, 3 SNAPSHOT_HEADER, 4 SNAPSHOT_FOOTER, 5 KRAFT_VERSION, 6 KRAFT_VOTERS, -1 UNKNOWN. Control records are never compacted and clients may not write them (ControlRecordType.java:43-57; LogValidator.java:476).

EndTransactionMarker is the value of COMMIT/ABORT control records and carries the coordinatorEpoch, version-prefix-serialized via the generated EndTxnMarker message (EndTransactionMarker.java:41-47,82-103).

Legacy v0/v1 (LegacyRecord) store a per-message CRC-32 (plain, not CRC32C), magic, attributes and full timestamp; RECORD_OVERHEAD_V0=14, RECORD_OVERHEAD_V1=22. An uncompressed legacy set is one message per offset; a compressed set is a single wrapper whose value holds inner messages with relative offsets. v0/v1 lack headers, idempotence/transactions, efficient counts, leader epoch, and zstd (LegacyRecord.java:45-74,508-512; AbstractLegacyRecordBatch.java:45-56,174-217,315-413).

All record formats share a 12-byte log overhead prefix (baseOffset:int64 + length:int32 = LOG_OVERHEAD=12); the magic byte sits at a format-independent offset 16 (MAGIC_OFFSET), so a reader parses the magic before interpreting the rest (Records.java:45-55).

MemoryRecordsBuilder writes the records (optionally through the compressor) after reserving the header region, then backfills the header and computes the CRC last on close(); it enforces strictly increasing offsets and bounds records/offset-delta by Integer.MAX_VALUE (MemoryRecordsBuilder.java:138-147,361-429,461-463,784-799).

FileLogInputStream.nextBatch() reads only 17 bytes (HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC = LOG_OVERHEAD+4+1) to learn offset/size/magic and builds a lazy FileChannelRecordBatch that materializes just the header or the full batch on demand, enabling zero-copy fetch (FileLogInputStream.java:34-94,194-222).

v2 preserves a batch's first/last offset and sequence even when records are removed by compaction (the cleaner may retain an empty header-only batch) so producer state can be rebuilt after leader failover without spurious OutOfOrderSequence errors (DefaultRecordBatch.java:73-95).

Producer knobs shaping batches: compression.type default none, batch.size default 16384, compression.gzip.level default DEFAULT_COMPRESSION, compression.lz4.level default 9, compression.zstd.level default 3 (ProducerConfig.java:82,244,250,254,258,409-413).

The current magic is RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE = MAGIC_VALUE_V2 = 2; the broker's LogValidator still understands v0/v1 and can up/down-convert incoming batches to a target toMagic for reading historical segments (RecordBatch.java:39-46; LogValidator.java:75,116,128-129).

title: The Wire Protocol & RPC Framework
abstract: A source-grounded reference on how Kafka frames and evolves its RPCs: the 4-byte length-prefixed frame, the derived-version request/response headers, the ApiKeys registry, the JSON-schema-driven message generator, and the flexible (compact + tagged-field) encoding from KIP-482. It explains the Readable/Writable/ByteBufferAccessor (de)serialization machinery, two-pass sizing with ObjectSerializationCache, zero-copy SendBuilder, version negotiation via ApiVersions (KIP-35/511), and the Errors code space — closing with a byte-by-byte layout of a real ApiVersionsRequest.
sections: Where It Lives in the Code
Reading and Writing: Readable / Writable / ByteBufferAccessor
FLEXIBLE Versions: Compact Encodings & Tagged Fields (KIP-482)
Worked Example: a Minimal ApiVersionsRequest on the Wire
keyFacts: A Kafka message on the wire is framed as a 4-byte big-endian int32 size (counting header+body only, not itself) followed by that many bytes; the size is read by NetworkReceive.readFrom (clients/.../network/NetworkReceive.java:84-101) and written as the first int32 by SendBuilder.buildSend (clients/.../protocol/SendBuilder.java:208-226).

The request header (schema RequestHeader.json, valid versions 1-2, flexible at 2+) carries RequestApiKey(int16), RequestApiVersion(int16), CorrelationId(int32), and ClientId(nullable string); ClientId is forced non-compact ('flexibleVersions':'none') even in header v2 so old brokers can always parse an ApiVersionsRequest header (RequestHeader.json:36-42).

The header version is never transmitted: it is derived from api key + api version. The generator emits requestHeaderVersion/responseHeaderVersion returning request header v2 if the API version is flexible else v1, and response header v1 if flexible else v0 (generator/.../ApiMessageTypeGenerator.java:346-389). RequestHeader.parse peeks the first two int16s to resolve it, then rewinds (requests/RequestHeader.java:123-152).

ApiVersionsResponse (api key 18) always uses a v0 response header (no tagged-field section) so a client that guessed the wrong version can still locate the body; this is hard-coded in the generator per KIP-511 (ApiMessageTypeGenerator.java:362-368).

ApiKeys is an enum spanning ids 0..84 (PRODUCE=0, FETCH=1, API_VERSIONS=18) ending at STREAMS_GROUP_TOPOLOGY_DESCRIPTION_UPDATE; each constant records id, name, clusterAction (broker-internal RPCs), forwardable (envelope/forwarding), and requiresDelayedAllocation (true when any schema version contains a bytes/records type) (clients/.../protocol/ApiKeys.java:48-207).

Removed APIs keep their ids reserved forever; ApiKeys.hasValidVersion() returns false when oldestVersion>latestVersion so the slot is never reused (ApiKeys.java:263-265). Produce v0-2 and Fetch v0-3 were removed in 4.0 with v3/v4 as new baselines.

In flexible versions (>= the schema's flexibleVersions floor), variable-length prefixes become compact unsigned varints written as length+1, reserving the value 0 for null; non-flexible uses int16 length for strings and int32 for bytes/arrays (generator/.../MessageDataGenerator.java:985-994, null branch at :946). Signed varints use zig-zag: (v<<1)^(v>>31) (utils/internals/ByteUtils.java:486-487).

Every struct in a flexible version ends with a tagged-field section: an unsigned varint count followed by entries of tag(uvarint), size(uvarint), value(size bytes), always serialized in the flexible form. Unknown tags are stored as RawTaggedField via Readable.readUnknownTaggedField and re-emitted, giving byte-for-byte round-trip forward compatibility (MessageDataGenerator.java:489-541, 794-879).

Serialization is a strict two-pass process sharing an ObjectSerializationCache (IdentityHashMap): pass 1 (addSize) computes the total size and caches derived values like UTF-8 string bytes and array sizes; pass 2 (write) emits bytes reusing them, guaranteeing the declared frame size equals bytes written (protocol/Message.java:49-76, protocol/ObjectSerializationCache.java).

SendBuilder provides zero-copy: writeByteBuffer/writeRecords flush accumulated bytes then append the records buffer by reference, returning a ByteBufferSend or MultiRecordsSend; this is why ApiKeys.requiresDelayedAllocation pins the receive buffer for records-bearing requests (protocol/SendBuilder.java:99-181).

Version negotiation (KIP-35): clients send ApiVersionsRequest at their highest known version; the broker replies with (ApiKey, MinVersion, MaxVersion) triples per supported API. ApiVersions is uniquely tolerant — ApiKeys.isVersionEnabled returns true for any version (ApiKeys.java:245-252) and on UNSUPPORTED_VERSION the broker answers as v0 still advertising its ApiVersions range (requests/ApiVersionsRequest.java:92-143).

For broker-to-controller forwarding, ApiVersionsResponse.intersectForwardableApis intersects broker and controller ranges per forwardable API; intersect = [max(minA,minB), min(maxA,maxB)] and is empty when ranges don't overlap (requests/ApiVersionsResponse.java:232-325).

Errors is an enum mapping a stable int16 code to an ApiException factory, with 137 entries from UNKNOWN_SERVER_ERROR(-1) and NONE(0) up to STREAMS_TOPOLOGY_DESCRIPTION_UPDATE_FAILED(135) (protocol/Errors.java:181-425). Error codes are immutable forever; only names/messages may change. forCode maps an unknown code to UNKNOWN_SERVER_ERROR with a warning (Errors.java:511-519), and forException walks the superclass chain (Errors.java:525-532).

AbstractRequest/AbstractResponse bridge the dynamic ApiKeys id to typed classes via large switch statements (doParseRequest at requests/AbstractRequest.java:172-363; parseResponse at requests/AbstractResponse.java:113-299), with a default branch throwing AssertionError to force wiring of new APIs. serializeWithHeader produces header+body without a size prefix; toSend includes it.

Each RPC's JSON schema (clients/src/main/resources/common/message/*.json) declares apiKey, listeners, validVersions, flexibleVersions, and a recursive fields tree with per-field versions, nullableVersions, taggedVersions+tag, ignorable, mapKey, entityType, and default; FieldType.parse recognizes bool/int8-64/uint16-32/uuid/float64/string/bytes/records/structs/[]arrays (generator/.../FieldType.java:386-432).

Throttling surfaces on the wire as a ThrottleTimeMs(int32) response field; AbstractResponse.shouldClientThrottle(version) defaults false and is overridden per-RPC so clients self-throttle when talking to newer brokers that send responses before throttling (requests/AbstractResponse.java:306-308).

title: The Log Storage Engine
abstract: A field-level reference for Kafka's per-partition log storage engine in the org.apache.kafka.storage.internals.log Java module. Covers the UnifiedLog → LocalLog → LogSegments → LogSegment layering, the three sparse memory-mapped indexes (offset/time/transaction) built on AbstractIndex, the exact append and read-by-offset algorithms, segment rolling, the on-disk directory layout, crash recovery via LogLoader, and the ProducerStateManager and LeaderEpochFileCache that supply idempotency/transaction state and truncation-safe epoch lineage.
sections: Where it lives in the code
On-disk directory layout of a partition
Data structures: the index files, field by field
Offset boundaries: LSO, LEO, HW, recovery point
keyFacts: A Kafka partition log is the layered stack UnifiedLog over LocalLog over LogSegments over LogSegment; LogSegments is a thread-safe ConcurrentSkipListMap keyed by segment base offset (LogSegments.java:42), and the active segment is simply the last (highest-base-offset) entry (LogSegments.java:322).

Each LogSegment is a FileRecords .log file paired with a lazily-mmapped OffsetIndex (.index), a lazily-mmapped TimeIndex (.timeindex), and a TransactionIndex (.txnindex), with a base offset that is a lower bound on offsets in the segment (LogSegment.java:80-103). Segment files are named by 20-digit zero-padded base offset (LogFileUtils.java:102).

Indexes are sparse and store offsets RELATIVE to the segment base offset so a 64-bit offset fits in 4 bytes: OffsetIndex entries are 8 bytes (relOffset int32, position int32; OffsetIndex.java:56), TimeIndex entries are 12 bytes (timestamp int64, relOffset int32; TimeIndex.java:56). An entry is added only every index.interval.bytes (default 4096) of log (LogSegment.java:271).

AbstractIndex uses a cache-friendly 'warm section' binary search over the last warmEntries()=8192/entrySize index slots so tailing followers/consumers avoid cold-page faults near the file start (AbstractIndex.java:387,495); the optimization was added because pure binary search caused ~1s produce-latency spikes (AbstractIndex.java:330-386).

Index concurrency: a ReentrantLock serializes mutations (append/truncate/resize) while readers take no such lock; a separate ReentrantReadWriteLock (remapLock) lets readers run concurrently but gives resize/unmap exclusive access to swap the MappedByteBuffer (AbstractIndex.java:59-61). Readers call mmap().duplicate() and only read committed data.

The leader append path runs entirely under UnifiedLog's synchronized(lock): LogValidator.validateMessagesAndAssignOffsets assigns offsets from the current LEO, stamps the leader epoch into each v2 batch header and validates/sets timestamps (LogValidator.java:123,209), then maybeRoll, then LocalLog.append writes bytes and advances LEO to lastOffset+1 (UnifiedLog.java:1115-1292, LocalLog.java:527).

A segment rolls when ANY of these holds (LogSegment.shouldRoll, LogSegment.java:168): adding the batch would exceed segment.bytes (default 1 GiB); the non-empty segment exceeded segment.ms (default 7 days) minus jitter; the offset or time index isFull(); or the next offset cannot be expressed relative to the base offset. TimeIndex.isFull reserves one slot (entries >= maxEntries-1) for the final on-roll entry (TimeIndex.java:122).

Read-by-offset translates an offset to a physical position via OffsetIndex.lookup (greatest indexed offset <= target) followed by a short forward scan in FileRecords.searchForOffsetFromPosition, returning a zero-copy FileRecords.slice (LogSegment.java:399-464); LocalLog.read walks successive segments via floorSegment/higherSegment until data is found (LocalLog.java:463).

Offset invariants enforced by UnifiedLog: logStartOffset <= lastStableOffset <= highWatermark <= logEndOffset, and localLogStartOffset >= logStartOffset; HW can never exceed LEO and only moves forward (UnifiedLog.java:564), and advancing log start offset requires it to be <= HW or throws OffsetOutOfRangeException (UnifiedLog.java:1358). HW/LSO are volatile for lock-free reads.

The recovery point is the first not-yet-fsynced offset and is advanced only at an actual flush (UnifiedLog.flush, UnifiedLog.java:2254); default flush.messages and flush.ms are both Long.MAX_VALUE, so durability relies on replication and OS page cache, not fsync. A clean-shutdown marker lets LogLoader skip recovery entirely (LogLoader.java:456-512).

LogLoader.load() runs multi-pass recovery: remove temp files and resolve .swap/.cleaned files (KAFKA-6264 split handling), load segments ascending, sanity-check, recover segments above the recovery point by replaying and rebuilding indexes/producer-state/epoch-cache and truncating the first invalid byte onward (LogSegment.recover, LogSegment.java:483-529), then rebuild producer state from the latest snapshot (LogLoader.java:128-251). LogSegment.sanityCheck skips offset/time-index checks since those segments are re-recovered anyway (LogSegment.java:181-192, KIP-263).

ProducerStateManager provides idempotency and transaction metadata: a per-producer-id map whose ProducerStateEntry retains up to NUM_BATCHES_TO_RETAIN=5 batches for duplicate detection (ProducerStateEntry.java:35), plus TreeMaps of ongoing/unreplicated transactions used to compute the last stable offset (lastStableOffset = first offset of next open txn, else completedTxn.lastOffset+1; ProducerStateManager.java:526).

Producer snapshot files named <lastOffset>.snapshot are taken on every roll; format is Version int16 @0, CRC uint32 @2 (Crc32C over bytes from offset 6 to EOF), then length-prefixed ProducerEntry records (ProducerId, Epoch, LastSequence, LastOffset, OffsetDelta, Timestamp, CoordinatorEpoch, CurrentTxnFirstOffset). A CRC mismatch raises CorruptSnapshotException (ProducerStateManager.java:618,664; ProducerSnapshot.json validVersions 1).

LeaderEpochFileCache maps each leader epoch to the first offset of that epoch (TreeMap guarded by a ReentrantReadWriteLock) and persists to the plain-text leader-epoch-checkpoint file (version, count, then 'epoch startOffset' lines). endOffsetFor returns the LEO for the current epoch or the next epoch's start offset otherwise, driving truncation-safe follower replication (LeaderEpochFileCache.java:284); truncations flush asynchronously since they run on replica-fetcher threads.

The TransactionIndex is NOT memory-mapped, is created lazily only when an abort is recorded, and appends 34-byte version-prefixed AbortedTxn records (ProducerId, FirstOffset, LastOffset, LastStableOffset; AbortedTxn.json validVersions 0). collectAbortedTxns returns aborted txns overlapping a fetch range and signals whether scanning must continue into the next segment (TransactionIndex.java:53,167).

Aborted-offset overflow recovery: a batch whose last offset exceeds Integer.MAX_VALUE relative to the segment base offset throws LogSegmentOffsetOverflowException, and LogLoader.retryOnOffsetOverflow splits the offending legacy segment and retries loading (LogLoader.java:326). Indexes carry no checksum and are rebuilt from the log on any CorruptIndexException.

title: Log Management, Retention & Compaction
abstract: Broker-wide log lifecycle: LogManager as the JBOD-aware registry of all UnifiedLogs, its parallel startup recovery and five background schedulers, and disk-failure handling via LogDirFailureChannel. Covers size/time retention (whole-segment oldest-first deletion with the async .deleted rename and file.delete.delay.ms) and log compaction (the LogCleaner thread pool, dirtiest-log selection, the two-pass Cleaner with a SkimpyOffsetMap, tombstone/delete.retention.ms handling, the crash-safe .cleaned/.swap swap, and transaction-aware cleaning), all coordinated by the per-partition LogCleaningState machine.
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: LogManager is the broker-wide registry of all UnifiedLogs, holding currentLogs and futureLogs as ConcurrentHashMaps and serializing creation/deletion/rename with a single logCreationOrDeletionLock monitor (LogManager.java:91-96,1270).

On startup LogManager.loadLogs recovers logs per-directory in parallel using a fixed pool of num.recovery.threads.per.data.dir threads (default 2) named 'log-recovery-<dir>-<n>'; if a CleanShutdownFileHandler marker exists it is deleted immediately and segment recovery is skipped, otherwise every segment above the recovery point is re-validated (LogManager.java:608,625,629-671).

LogManager schedules five background tasks after log.initial.task.delay.ms (default 30000ms): kafka-log-retention (every log.retention.check.interval.ms, default 300000ms), kafka-log-flusher, kafka-recovery-point-checkpoint, kafka-log-start-offset-checkpoint, and the self-rescheduling one-shot kafka-delete-logs (LogManager.java:811-833).

Retention deletes whole oldest-first segments via UnifiedLog.deleteOldSegments(), running three predicates in order — logStartOffset breach, retention.bytes breach, then retention.ms breach (now - segment.largestTimestamp() > retentionMs); a segment is deletable only if highWatermark() >= its upper-bound offset, and the log always keeps at least one segment (UnifiedLog.java:1967-2092,1892-1942).

Defaults: retention.ms=604800000 (7 days), retention.bytes=-1 (unbounded), file.delete.delay.ms=60000, delete.retention.ms=86400000 (24h), min.cleanable.dirty.ratio=0.5, min.compaction.lag.ms=0, max.compaction.lag.ms=Long.MAX_VALUE (LogConfig.java:134-138; ServerLogConfigs LOG_RETENTION_BYTES_DEFAULT=-1, LOG_DELETE_DELAY_MS_DEFAULT=60000).

Segment deletion is two-phase and crash-safe: LocalLog.deleteSegmentFiles renames .log/.index/.timeindex/.txnindex to *.deleted and removes them from the in-memory index immediately, then schedules the physical unlink file.delete.delay.ms later via a 'delete-file' scheduler task; leftover .deleted files are cleaned on recovery (LocalLog.java:846-877,982-983).

Compaction runs in a dedicated pool of log.cleaner.threads (default 1) ShutdownableThreads named 'kafka-log-cleaner-thread-<id>'; each loops grabbing the filthiest log, cleaning it, then deleting eligible segments, sleeping log.cleaner.backoff.ms (default 15000ms) when nothing is eligible (LogCleaner.java:467-523).

The cleaner selects the log with the maximum cleanableRatio = cleanableBytes/(cleanBytes+cleanableBytes); a log is eligible if that ratio exceeds min.cleanable.dirty.ratio OR needCompactionNow (max.compaction.lag.ms exceeded); the first-uncleanable offset is min(lastStableOffset, activeSegment.baseOffset, first segment younger than min.compaction.lag.ms) (LogCleanerManager.java:281-296,730-742; LogToClean.java:45-54).

Pass 1 builds a SkimpyOffsetMap of key->highest-offset over the dirty range using an open-addressing hash table that stores a 16-byte MD5 of the key plus an 8-byte offset (24 bytes/slot), capped at slots*loadFactor (default 0.9) entries; the per-thread buffer is log.cleaner.dedupe.buffer.size/threads (default 128MiB total), capped at 2GiB (SkimpyOffsetMap.java:32-88; Cleaner.java:710-817,480).

Pass 2 recopies segments keeping a record only if it has the latest offset for its key AND (has a value OR is a tombstone still within its delete horizon); records with no key are dropped as invalid; segments are grouped by segment.bytes/index size to avoid shrinking, written to *.cleaned, and swapped via the crash-safe .cleaned->.swap->live sequence with descending-offset renames (Cleaner.java:567-602,641-676; LocalLog.java:1004-1062).

Tombstones (null-value records) are retained for delete.retention.ms after entering the clean section; for v2 records this is enforced by stamping a delete-horizon into the batch header and dropping only once currentTime >= batch.deleteHorizonMs() (Cleaner.java:166-170,589-596).

Compaction is transaction-aware: it never cleans past the last stable offset, drops aborted-transaction records unconditionally, retains the last batch of each active producer (RETAIN_EMPTY) for sequence continuity, retains the batch whose next offset equals the high watermark, and keeps transaction markers until their transaction's records are gone (LogCleaner.java:81-96; Cleaner.java:386-420).

A per-partition LogCleaningState machine (sealed: InProgress / Aborted / Paused(n)) under a single ReentrantLock in LogCleanerManager prevents concurrent retention+compaction; truncation, intra-broker moves, and deletion call abortAndPauseCleaning then resumeCleaning, with reentrant Paused(n) counts (LogCleaningState.java:24-72; LogCleanerManager.java:367-421).

Disk failure flows from any I/O thread through LogDirFailureChannel (a bounded ArrayBlockingQueue sized to the dir count, each dir enqueued at most once) to the single LogDirFailureHandler thread in ReplicaManager, which calls LogManager.handleLogDirFailure to drop the dir, stop cleaning it, mark its partitions offline, and halt the broker only if it was the last live dir (LogDirFailureChannel.java:43-65; ReplicaManager.scala:227-232; LogManager.java:411-444).

The three offset checkpoints (recovery-point-offset-checkpoint, log-start-offset-checkpoint, cleaner-offset-checkpoint) share one plain-text format (version line, count line, 'topic partition offset' triples) and are written crash-consistently via Utils.atomicMoveWithFallback through CheckpointFileWithFailureHandler (OffsetCheckpointFile.java:40-50; LogManager.java:1054; LogCleanerManager.java:66).

log.cleaner.enable (default true) is deprecated since 4.1 and removed in 5.0 — the cleaner will always run; LogCleaner implements BrokerReconfigurable and reconfiguration shuts down and recreates the thread pool, also resurrecting any dead cleaner threads (CleanerConfig.java:53-54; LogCleaner.java:289-301,838-840).

title: Tiered Storage (Remote Log)
abstract: Reference chapter on Kafka tiered storage covering the RSM and RLMM SPIs, the RemoteLogManager tasks, the remote read path, RemoteIndexCache, the default metadata topic backed RLMM, and the copy and fetch quotas.
keyFacts: Enabled by remote.log.storage.system.enable and orchestrated by RemoteLogManager
RSM moves bytes idempotently and RLMM stores metadata strongly consistently
Only FINISHED segments are readable
copyLogSegment writes STARTED then RSM copies then writes FINISHED
Local deletion is guarded by highestOffsetInRemoteStorage
Upload eligibility is a sealed segment below last stable offset gated by copy lag
Expiration enforces remote retention within the leader epoch lineage
Remote reads run on a bounded reader pool via DelayedRemoteFetch
RemoteIndexCache is an on disk caffeine cache with two phase eviction
Default RLMM uses the internal remote log metadata topic with fifty partitions
Read your writes blocks producing until the consumer task catches up
Copy quota back pressures the task and fetch quota sheds reads

title: Network Layer & Threading Model
abstract: A field-level walkthrough of the Kafka broker network stack and broker thread anatomy: SocketServer with per-listener Acceptor and Processor (NIO reactor) threads, the shared common.network.Selector/KafkaChannel reactor with size-delimited NetworkReceive framing, the bounded RequestChannel hand-off queue, the KafkaRequestHandler I/O-thread pool dispatching to KafkaApis, ConnectionQuotas, and the request purgatory (DelayedOperationPurgatory + hierarchical TimingWheel). Closes with the full request-lifecycle timing model and a census of broker thread types. Everything is cited to Apache Kafka 4.4.0-SNAPSHOT (KRaft) source.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The request purgatory & hierarchical timing wheel
keyFacts: A Kafka broker's network stack is a hand-rolled NIO reactor: per-listener Acceptor threads accept sockets and round-robin them to N Processor threads (num.network.threads, default 3, PER LISTENER), each running its own java.nio Selector; requests are handed off via a bounded RequestChannel to M KafkaRequestHandler threads (num.io.threads, default 8) that call KafkaApis.handle (core/src/main/scala/kafka/network/SocketServer.scala:801, server/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/server/config/ServerConfigs.java:50).

In KRaft 4.x the control-plane listener has been removed; SocketServer models only dataPlaneAcceptors (SocketServer.scala:103). Controller traffic is isolated by a separate controller process running its own SocketServer with ListenerType.CONTROLLER and its own request-handler pool named 'controller' (SocketServer.scala:150, ControllerServer.scala:300); KIP-291 originated this separation.

RequestChannel is the back-pressure point: a bounded ArrayBlockingQueue of size queued.max.requests (default 500); sendRequest does a blocking put, so when handlers fall behind, network threads block and stop reading sockets, converting handler slowness into TCP back-pressure (RequestChannel.scala:90, :117).

Per-connection request ordering is guaranteed by two rules: Selector adds AT MOST ONE completed receive per channel per poll(), and the Processor mutes the channel the instant a request is enqueued, un-muting only after the response is sent (Selector.java:434, SocketServer.scala:1044). This holds even though requests fan out to many handler threads.

Requests are size-delimited: a 4-byte big-endian size N followed by N payload bytes; the broker rejects N<0 or N>socket.request.max.bytes (default 100 MiB) with InvalidReceiveException, and allocates the payload from a MemoryPool — pool exhaustion mutes the channel as back-pressure for queued.max.request.bytes (NetworkReceive.java:82-95, KafkaChannel.java:407-418, Selector.java:457).

The request purgatory (DelayedOperationPurgatory) parks operations that can't complete immediately; tryCompleteElseWatch holds the operation's ReentrantLock across 'add-to-watchlist + recheck' to avoid missing concurrent triggering events, then adds it to the timeout timer. Watchers are sharded into 512 lists by abs(key.hashCode()%512) to reduce lock contention (DelayedOperationPurgatory.java:41, :122).

The timeout side is a hierarchical TimingWheel (SystemTimer default tickMs=1, wheelSize=20): insert is O(1) within a level / O(m) across overflow levels, cancel is O(1) (unlink + AtomicInteger decrement). Buckets are driven by a shared DelayQueue<TimerTaskList>; this design (KAFKA-1989) replaced the old O(log n) DelayQueue purgatory that risked OOM from un-purged completed ops (TimingWheel.java:97-179, SystemTimer.java:45).

The broker creates several purgatories keyed off ReplicaManager: Produce, Fetch, DeleteRecords, RemoteFetch (purge interval forced to 0 to free ~50 MiB RemoteLogReadResult buffers immediately), RemoteListOffsets, and ShareFetch; coordinators add DelayedJoin/DelayedHeartbeat and transaction purgatories (ReplicaManager.scala:184-208).

ConnectionQuotas enforces per-IP (max.connections.per.ip, default Integer.MAX_VALUE), per-listener, and broker-wide (max.connections) caps plus a connection-creation-rate limit (max.connection.creation.rate), all guarded by synchronizing on the counts map with wait/notifyAll; the inter-broker listener is 'protected' (exempt from the broker cap, closing an LRU connection elsewhere instead) (SocketServer.scala:1276, :1474). Quota enforcement runs ON the single acceptor thread and can block accepts for a whole listener.

Request latency is decomposed via seven nanosecond timestamps on Request into requestQueueTime, localTime, remoteTime (= purgatory wait), responseQueueTime, responseSendTime, plus throttleTime; each is written by a different actor (network thread, handler thread, RequestChannel.sendResponse, Processor.dequeueResponse) and rendered per-API by updateRequestMetrics (server/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/network/Request.java:325, KafkaRequestHandler.scala:164).

Broker network thread types and naming: data-plane-kafka-socket-acceptor-* (1/listener, non-daemon), data-plane-kafka-network-thread-* (num.network.threads/listener, non-daemon), data-plane-kafka-request-handler-N (num.io.threads, DAEMON), ExpirationReaper-<brokerId>-<Purgatory> (1/purgatory, non-daemon, ticks advanceClock(200)), and executor-<Purgatory> (1/SystemTimer, runs expired TimerTasks) (SocketServer.scala:510,750; KafkaRequestHandler.scala:275; DelayedOperationPurgatory.java:409; SystemTimer.java:31).

The shared reactor lives in clients/ (common.network) and is used verbatim by producers, consumers, replica fetchers and the broker accept side: Selector (not thread-safe, one per Processor) + KafkaChannel (per-connection mute state machine) + TransportLayer (PlaintextTransportLayer/SslTransportLayer) + Authenticator; the broker side never touches sockets from handler threads — responses route back to the originating Processor's responseQueue (Selector.java:88, KafkaChannel.java:67, RequestChannel.scala:189).

title: Request Processing (KafkaApis)
abstract: How a Kafka broker turns a parsed request into a response: the request-handler thread pool dequeues from a shared RequestChannel and runs KafkaApis.handle, a giant match on apiKey that dispatches to ~90 handlers, each of which authorizes, validates, acts (often via ReplicaManager or a coordinator, frequently parking the work in a purgatory), builds a response, applies quota throttling, and sends it back through the originating network processor. The chapter walks Produce (→ DelayedProduce for acks=all), Fetch (→ DelayedFetch), Metadata (→ MetadataCache), and coordinator-delegating APIs end to end, and covers the ApiVersionManager, the broker/controller (ControllerApis) split, authorization, throttling, error mapping, and the concurrency model that keeps handler threads non-blocking.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The request-handler thread & the RequestChannel round-trip
Broker APIs vs. controller APIs (the split)
keyFacts: KafkaApis.handle (core/src/main/scala/kafka/server/KafkaApis.scala:155) dispatches every broker request via a single Scala 'match' on request.header.apiKey covering roughly 90 API keys; a missing key throws IllegalStateException and a defensive apiVersionManager.isApiEnabled check (line 166) gates unsupported versions.

Request-handler threads are named 'data-plane-kafka-request-handler-N' (KafkaRequestHandler.scala:275); the pool size is num.io.threads (default 8, ServerConfigs.NUM_IO_THREADS_DEFAULT) and is dynamically resizable via KafkaRequestHandlerPool.resizeThreadPool.

RequestChannel (RequestChannel.scala:80) holds an ArrayBlockingQueue 'requestQueue' of capacity queued.max.requests (default 500, SocketServerConfigs.QUEUED_MAX_REQUESTS_DEFAULT), a ConcurrentHashMap of processors for response routing, and a separate callbackQueue for rescheduled completions that jump the queue.

Each request emits exactly one terminal RequestChannel response: SendResponse, NoOpResponse (e.g. acks=0 produce), or CloseConnectionResponse; StartThrottlingResponse/EndThrottlingResponse mute the channel additionally without consuming the real response (RequestChannel.scala:42-77,174-187).

For acks=all produce, ReplicaManager writes locally then calls delayedProducePurgatory.tryCompleteElseWatch with a DelayedProduce keyed by TopicPartitionOperationKey (ReplicaManager.scala:903-911); DelayedProduce pre-sets succeeded partitions to REQUEST_TIMED_OUT and clears them only when enough replicas reach the required offset (DelayedProduce.java:111-121,140).

handleFetchRequest builds a FetchContext via FetchManager.newContext (incremental fetch sessions), authorizes followers with CLUSTER_ACTION and consumers with READ per topic, then calls replicaManager.fetchMessages; under-min-bytes reads park in DelayedFetch which re-evaluates 8 completion cases including diverging-epoch truncation (DelayedFetch.scala:70-146).

Follower fetches and Raft RPCs are sent exempt from client throttling (RequestHandlerHelper.sendResponseExemptThrottle) but recorded against the leader replication quota; consumer fetch and produce apply max(bandwidthThrottle, requestThrottle) and mute the channel, with the request quota skipped entirely for acks=0 (KafkaApis.scala:494-505).

ApiVersionManager.isApiEnabled = apiKey.inScope(listenerType) AND apiKey.isVersionEnabled(version, enableUnstableLastVersion) (ApiVersionManager.java:62-64); ApiVersions itself is accepted at any version so clients can negotiate down to v0 (ApiKeys.java:245-252).

The broker uses DefaultApiVersionManager (built BrokerServer.scala:267) which intersects broker APIs with the active controller's NodeApiVersions so clients never attempt a forwardable admin API at a version the controller cannot service; the KRaft controller uses SimpleApiVersionManager (ControllerServer.scala:162).

Brokers do not mutate cluster metadata: ~15 admin APIs (CREATE_TOPICS, DELETE_TOPICS, CREATE/DELETE_ACLS, ALTER_CONFIGS family, ALTER_CLIENT_QUOTAS, ELECT_LEADERS, reassignments, UPDATE_FEATURES, Raft-voter APIs, DESCRIBE_QUORUM) are dispatched to forwardToController, wrapped in an Envelope and re-dispatched by ControllerApis.handle (KafkaApis.scala:188-237, ControllerApis.scala:96-137,164).

Authorization runs through AuthHelper; filterByAuthorized issues ONE batched Authorizer call for all of a request's topics/partitions (AuthHelper.scala:100) and a missing/None authorizer allows everything; partition-level denials become per-resource error codes while cluster denials throw ClusterAuthorizationException mapped to CLUSTER_AUTHORIZATION_FAILED.

Asynchronous completions that need the originating thread's RequestLocal are rescheduled onto a request-handler thread via KafkaRequestHandler.wrapAsyncCallback, which enqueues a CallbackRequest plus a WakeupRequest on the RequestChannel (KafkaRequestHandler.scala:65-85,131-160).

Group/transaction/share APIs (OFFSET_COMMIT, JOIN_GROUP, CONSUMER_GROUP_HEARTBEAT, SHARE_*, STREAMS_*) return CompletableFuture[Unit] and the dispatcher attaches .exceptionally(handleError); InitProducerId and AddPartitionsToTxn down-convert PRODUCER_FENCED to INVALID_PRODUCER_EPOCH for old clients (KafkaApis.scala:1643-1646,1955-1958).

Produce and other requests whose schema retains record bytes are flagged ApiKeys.requiresDelayedAllocation (forwardable OR schema has BYTES/NULLABLE_BYTES; ApiKeys.java:194) so their network buffer is held until processing completes and released in the handler loop's finally (KafkaRequestHandler.scala:175, Request.java:113-115).

The RequestHandlerAvgIdlePercent meter (KafkaRequestHandler.scala:111-123,253) is the canonical handler-pool saturation signal; the Request object carries volatile timing fields (requestDequeue/apiLocalComplete/responseComplete/responseDequeue) feeding per-API histograms, with metric-name overrides distinguishing FetchFollower vs FetchConsumer and verify-only AddPartitionsToTxn (Request.java:339-356).

title: Replication, ISR & High Watermark
abstract: A source-grounded reference chapter on Kafka's KRaft-mode replication subsystem: how ReplicaManager materialises metadata into Partition state machines (applyDelta/makeLeader/makeFollower), how the leader tracks each follower via Replica/ReplicaState, how the ISR shrinks and expands and is committed controller-authoritatively through the AlterPartition RPC, how the high watermark advances as the minimum LEO over the maximal ISR, how leader epochs drive epoch-based truncation, and how acks/min.insync.replicas/unclean-election/ELR shape durability. Verified against the actual Scala/Java source.
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: In KRaft the ISR is controller-authoritative: a leader proposes ISR changes via the AlterPartition RPC (apiKey 56, valid versions 2-3, targets the controller listener) and the controller commits them by appending a PartitionChangeRecord to the Raft metadata log and bumping the partition epoch (Partition.scala:1687, AlterPartitionRequest.json).

The high watermark equals the minimum log-end-offset over the maximal ISR and never advances while the ISR is smaller than the effective min.insync.replicas; it is computed in Partition.maybeIncrementLeaderHW (Partition.scala:1010) and is monotonic and never exceeds the leader LEO (UnifiedLog.java:563).

A follower's in-sync status hinges on ReplicaState.lastCaughtUpTimeMs; ReplicaState.isCaughtUp returns true iff follower LEO == leader LEO or now - lastCaughtUpTimeMs <= replica.lag.time.max.ms (ReplicaState.java:62), covering both stuck and slow followers.

The ISR is modelled as a state machine: CommittedPartitionState (maximalIsr==isr), PendingExpandIsr (maximalIsr = isr + new replica, optimistic) and PendingShrinkIsr (maximalIsr = committed isr, pessimistic); the asymmetry guarantees a rejected AlterPartition can never have prematurely advanced the HW (PendingExpandIsr.java:43, PendingShrinkIsr.java:41).

A replica may only join the ISR once its LEO reaches BOTH the leader's high watermark AND the current leader-epoch start offset, and only if it is unfenced, not in controlled shutdown, and its Fetch broker epoch matches the metadata-cached broker epoch (Partition.isFollowerInSync at Partition.scala:907, isReplicaIsrEligible at Partition.scala:914 — KIP-841/KIP-903).

acks=all (acks=-1) rejects the append up front with NotEnoughReplicasException if ISR < effective min ISR (Partition.scala:1234), then parks a DelayedProduce that completes when leaderLog.highWatermark >= the produced offset; if the ISR shrank below min ISR meanwhile it completes with NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS_AFTER_APPEND (Partition.checkEnoughReplicasReachOffset, Partition.scala:947).

Leader epochs enable precise follower truncation: each replica keeps a LeaderEpochFileCache (TreeMap of epoch->startOffset); endOffsetFor(requestedEpoch, logEndOffset) returns the start offset of the next-higher epoch as the divergence pivot, answering OffsetsForLeaderEpoch (LeaderEpochFileCache.java:284 — KIP-101/KIP-279).

DefaultAlterPartitionManager batches ISR updates with at most one pending item per partition (ConcurrentHashMap unsentIsrUpdates) and one in-flight RPC at a time (AtomicBoolean inflightRequest); the request is never timed out and retries indefinitely until answered or superseded, with a 50ms backoff on top-level errors (DefaultAlterPartitionManager.java:108,118,138,174).

The partition epoch is the optimistic-concurrency token: an AlterPartition commits only when its partitionEpoch matches the controller's current value, else it is rejected with INVALID_UPDATE_VERSION; INELIGIBLE_REPLICA and OPERATION_NOT_ATTEMPTED are authoritative 'not applied' signals that let the leader reset to last-committed state (Partition.handleAlterPartitionError, Partition.scala:1733).

Config defaults: replica.lag.time.max.ms=30000 (ReplicationConfigs.java:55), min.insync.replicas=1 capped at min(value, replicationFactor) via effectiveMinIsr (Partition.scala:246), unclean.leader.election.enable=false (LogConfig.java:139), replica.high.watermark.checkpoint.interval.ms=5000 (ReplicationConfigs.java:103); the isr-expiration sweep runs every replica.lag.time.max.ms/2 so a follower can lag up to ~1.5x before eviction (ReplicaManager.scala:285).

Eligible Leader Replicas (KIP-966) add elr[] and lastKnownElr[] to PartitionRegistration (PartitionRegistration.java:158); the controller's PartitionChangeBuilder elects from the ISR first, then from the ELR when the ISR is empty (isValidNewLeader at PartitionChangeBuilder.java:318), then lastKnownElr, and only as an unclean last resort an arbitrary acceptable assigned replica (electAnyLeader, PartitionChangeBuilder.java:256), tolerating up to min.insync.replicas-1 unclean shutdowns without committed-data loss.

On a follower, ReplicaManager.applyLocalFollowersDelta always calls makeFollower even when the leader is unavailable so the local replica and its HW checkpoint exist (KAFKA-1647), and a fetcher is (re)started only when the leader epoch changed, beginning at initialFetchOffset = own LEO if the epoch cache is non-empty else the high watermark (ReplicaManager.scala:2093,2449,2473).

title: The Fetch Path & Replica Fetchers
abstract: Kafka has one read path served by ReplicaManager.fetchMessages, walked by two clients: follower brokers replicating from a leader and consumers draining committed data. The chapter dissects the follower side (the AbstractFetcherThread truncate→build-fetch→append→advance-HW loop, work partitioning across num.replica.fetchers threads keyed by (leader, fetcherId), the leader-epoch truncation handshake, tiered-storage handling, and intra-broker JBOD moves via ReplicaAlterLogDirsThread) and the leader-serving side (log reads bounded by HW/LSO/LOG_END isolation, DelayedFetch purgatory waiting on min.bytes/max.wait.ms, incremental fetch sessions per KIP-227, rack-aware fetch-from-follower per KIP-392, and zero-copy sendfile via FileRecords). Every claim is grounded in the 4.4.0-SNAPSHOT source.
sections: Where it lives in the code
Tiered-storage interplay on the fetch path
keyFacts: Follower replication is a fetch loop: a follower is a client of its leader's Fetch API, and the same ReplicaManager.fetchMessages (core/src/main/scala/kafka/server/ReplicaManager.scala:1665) serves both followers and consumers, distinguished only by FetchParams.replicaId being a valid broker id vs the consumer sentinel (FetchParams.java:67).

AbstractFetcherThread.doWork() repeats maybeTruncate() then maybeFetch() (AbstractFetcherThread.scala:112); ReplicaFetcherThread adds completeDelayedFetchRequests() (ReplicaFetcherThread.scala:107). It runs as a ShutdownableThread looping while isRunning() (ShutdownableThread.java:131).

Work is partitioned across threads by fetcherId = Utils.abs(31*topic.hashCode + partition) % num.replica.fetchers, keyed by (leader broker id, fetcherId) in AbstractFetcherManager.fetcherThreadMap (AbstractFetcherManager.scala:113,40). With the default num.replica.fetchers=1 a follower runs one fetcher thread per source leader broker.

A follower always fetches at exactly its log-end offset (asserted in ReplicaFetcherThread.processPartitionData:124) and sets its high watermark strictly from the leader's reported HW via log.maybeUpdateHighWatermark (ReplicaFetcherThread.scala:143), so the follower HW never exceeds the leader's and its log is a committed prefix.

Truncation-on-fetch (KIP-595/KIP-320): RemoteLeaderEndPoint.isTruncationOnFetchSupported=true (:68); the follower sends lastFetchedEpoch in each FetchRequest.PartitionData and, when the response carries a diverging epoch, calls truncateOnFetchResponse with no extra round-trip (AbstractFetcherThread.scala:356,471). The fallback OffsetsForLeaderEpoch handshake computes the truncation offset in getOffsetTruncationState (AbstractFetcherThread.scala:604).

Read isolation is enforced on the leader at read time via FetchIsolation (FetchIsolation.java:31): followers read up to LOG_END, read_uncommitted consumers up to HIGH_WATERMARK, read_committed consumers up to the last stable offset (TXN_COMMITTED) — a consumer cannot observe data past these bounds.

DelayedFetch in the fetch purgatory completes when accumulated bytes reach params.minBytes (Case G, DelayedFetch.scala:142) or max.wait.ms expires, and force-completes early on leadership loss, offline dir, fenced epoch, segment roll, or a diverging epoch appearing while parked (Cases A–H, DelayedFetch.scala:70-122).

A parked consumer fetch is woken either by a produce advancing the HW (delayedFetchPurgatory.checkAndComplete, ReplicaManager.scala:864) or by the leader's own follower-replication progress: ReplicaFetcherThread collects partitionsWithNewHighWatermark and calls completeDelayedFetchRequests at loop end (ReplicaFetcherThread.scala:166).

Incremental fetch sessions (KIP-227): FetchManager.newContext (FetchManager.java:58) requires the request epoch to equal the stored session epoch (else INVALID_FETCH_SESSION_EPOCH) and bumps it via nextEpoch; CachedPartition.maybeUpdateResponseData (FetchSession.java:336) includes a partition in the response only if it has new records, changed HW/logStartOffset, an error, a diverging epoch, or a preferred-replica nomination.

The leader-side FetchSessionCache holds max.incremental.fetch.session.cache.slots sessions (default 1000) split across NumFetchSessionCacheShards=8 shards (KafkaBroker.scala:79, BrokerServer.scala:465). Eviction (FetchSessionCacheShard.tryEvict:204) first drops sessions idle > 120000 ms, then evicts unprivileged-before-privileged, so consumer sessions are evicted before follower sessions.

Fetch-from-follower (KIP-392): with replica.selector.class set (default null = leader only), findPreferredReadReplica (ReplicaManager.scala:1964) builds a view of the leader plus only in-sync remote replicas whose logEndOffset>=fetchOffset>=logStartOffset and asks the selector; the default RackAwareReplicaSelector returns the most caught-up same-rack replica. The leader then returns no data, only a preferredReadReplica id (ReplicaManager.scala:1806).

Records are returned as FileRecords (a window over the segment FileChannel); FileRecords.writeTo (FileRecords.java:291) calls destChannel.transferFrom, bottoming out in sendfile(2) so bytes flow from page cache to socket without the JVM heap. Followers convert FileRecords to MemoryRecords only because they must re-append (AbstractFetcherThread.scala:910).

ReplicaAlterLogDirsThread reuses the fetcher framework for intra-broker JBOD moves, fetching from a LocalLeaderEndPoint that calls ReplicaManager.fetchMessages in-process with maxWait=minBytes=0 (LocalLeaderEndPoint.scala:262); it copies one partition at a time (:207) and promotes the future replica via DirectoryEventHandler reassignment states (KIP-858).

All mutation of per-partition PartitionStates happens under partitionMapLock (a ReentrantLock with partitionMapCond), but the blocking network fetch in processFetchRequest runs without the lock and re-acquires it to apply results, re-validating that each partition's fetchOffset and currentLeaderEpoch are unchanged before updating state (AbstractFetcherThread.scala:348).

Key config defaults: replica.fetch.max.bytes=1 MiB, replica.fetch.response.max.bytes=10 MiB, replica.fetch.wait.max.ms=500, replica.fetch.min.bytes=1, replica.fetch.backoff.ms=1000, num.replica.fetchers=1 (ReplicationConfigs.java); the consumer-side fetch.max.bytes default is 55 MiB (ServerConfigs.java:106).

title: KRaft Consensus (Raft)
abstract: A deep reference chapter on Kafka's own Raft implementation (the metadata quorum). It dissects KafkaRaftClient as a single-threaded, non-blocking poll loop; the seven-role QuorumState machine (Unattached, Voted-as-Unattached, Prospective/pre-vote, Candidate, Follower, Leader, Resigned) and the durable quorum-state file; leader election with KIP-996 pre-vote; pull-based replication where followers/observers Fetch from a leader that never pushes; the majority high-watermark commit rule with the epoch-start restriction; KIP-630 snapshots; and KIP-853 dynamic quorum reconfiguration. Every claim is cited to the raft/ module source.
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: KRaft replicates the cluster metadata as a single topic partition (__cluster_metadata-0) over a controller voter quorum; ZooKeeper was removed entirely as of Kafka 4.0. The core class is KafkaRaftClient<T> implementing RaftClient<T> (raft/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/raft/KafkaRaftClient.java).

Replication is pull-based: followers and observers send Fetch (and FetchSnapshot) requests to the leader, which never pushes data. BeginQuorumEpoch (ApiKey 53) substitutes for classic Raft's heartbeat to let voters discover a new leader's id and endpoints (KafkaRaftClient.java:144).

KafkaRaftClient is driven by a single thread named 'kafka-raft-io-thread' (KafkaRaftClientDriver extends ShutdownableThread, name = threadNamePrefix + '-io-thread', default prefix 'kafka-raft'); its doWork() loops calling client.poll(), and nearly all Raft state is mutated only on this thread (KafkaRaftClientDriver.java:55).

The role state machine has seven states managed by QuorumState (one volatile EpochState). There is no separate VotedState; a binding vote is an UnattachedState (or ProspectiveState) carrying a non-empty votedKey (QuorumState.java:99, :428).

Each election increments the epoch by exactly one (QuorumState.transitionToCandidate: newEpoch = epoch()+1, QuorumState.java:641). Election timeouts are randomized as electionTimeoutMs + random.nextInt(electionTimeoutMs) (QuorumState.java:745).

Pre-vote (KIP-996): on a fetch timeout a voter first enters Prospective and sends Vote with PreVote=true at the current epoch (VoteRequest.json v2); only after a majority grant the pre-vote does it bump the epoch and become Candidate. A follower may grant a pre-vote even with a known leader if it has not yet successfully fetched from that leader (FollowerState.java:228).

The high watermark = the offset replicated to a majority of voters (the leader counts itself): LeaderState sorts ReplicaStates by descending end offset and takes index voterStates.size()/2 (LeaderState.java:727). A new leader cannot advance the HWM past prior-epoch records until it commits a record from its own epoch (epochStartOffset restriction, LeaderState.java:747); to force this it appends a LeaderChange control record on election (KafkaRaftClient.java:667).

Election state is persisted durably in a JSON file named 'quorum-state' before any in-memory transition: FileQuorumStateStore writes a .tmp file, fsyncs via getFD().sync(), then atomically renames (FileQuorumStateStore.java:181). Schema QuorumStateData has versions 0-1; v1 adds VotedDirectoryId (KIP-853) and drops the in-file CurrentVoters list.

Under kraft.version 1 the voter set lives in the log as VotersRecord control records (id, directory uuid, endpoints, supported-version range), tracked by KRaftControlRecordStateMachine/VoterSetHistory; kraft.version is KRAFT_VERSION_0 (no reconfig) or KRAFT_VERSION_1 (isReconfigSupported()==true, server-common KRaftVersion.java:59).

Log reconciliation rides on the Fetch response: a follower truncates to a leader-supplied diverging epoch/offset (never below its own HWM), or switches to FetchSnapshot when its offset is below the leader's log start (KafkaRaftClient.java:1742, :1765).

Snapshots (KIP-630) are files named '<20-digit-offset>-<10-digit-epoch>.checkpoint' alongside log segments; they embed SnapshotHeader, KRaftVersionRecord, VotersRecord and a SnapshotFooter. FetchSnapshot (ApiKey 59) transfers them by (snapshotId, byte position) as UnalignedRecords. The bootstrap snapshot id is (offset=0, epoch=0) and is never replicated (Snapshots.java:49,63).

Snapshot generation cadence is governed by metadata.log.max.snapshot.interval.ms (default 1 hour) and metadata.log.max.record.bytes.between.snapshots (default 20 MiB) (MetadataLogConfig.java:38). A 60-second timer calls KafkaRaftLog.maybeClean(), which always retains at least two snapshots (KafkaRaftLog.java:538).

Dynamic reconfiguration (KIP-853) changes one voter at a time via AddRaftVoter/RemoveRaftVoter/UpdateRaftVoter. AddVoter requires the leader's epoch to be committed (HWM established), the prior VotersRecord committed, the new voter to support the finalized kraft.version, and the new voter to be caught up (fetched within the last hour), then waits for the new VotersRecord to commit under the new majority (AddVoterHandler.java:45).

A leader resigns if it doesn't receive fetches from a majority of voters within 1.5 x controller.quorum.fetch.timeout.ms (check-quorum, CHECK_QUORUM_TIMEOUT_FACTOR=1.5, LeaderState.java:65); a single-voter quorum never expires this timer. On resignation it sends EndQuorumEpoch with preferred successors ordered by descending fetch offset.

Key default timeouts (controller.quorum.* in QuorumConfig.java): election.timeout.ms=1000, fetch.timeout.ms=2000, election.backoff.max.ms=1000, append.linger.ms=25, request.timeout.ms=2000, retry.backoff.ms=20, fetch.max.bytes=1048576; auto.join.enable defaults to false. Leader batches are bounded by MAX_BATCH_SIZE_BYTES=8 MiB and MAX_NUMBER_OF_BATCHES=10.

Concurrency boundaries are narrow: inbound RPCs/responses arrive on a BlockingMessageQueue (LinkedBlockingQueue) with wakeup(); leader appends use BatchAccumulator.appendLock (ReentrantLock) with an AtomicLong linger timer; resignation is a volatile flag; kraft.version upgrades and the control-record histories use AtomicReference CAS / object-monitor synchronization (BatchAccumulator.java:73, KRaftControlRecordStateMachine.java:62).

title: The KRaft Controller
abstract: The KRaft controller is a deterministic replicated state machine over the cluster metadata log. The QuorumController runs a single-threaded KafkaEventQueue loop that converts each request into a list of metadata records, appends them to the Raft log, and applies them to in-memory state under a strict write→commit→apply discipline, keeping the active controller and standbys byte-identical. The chapter covers the event loop, reversible timeline/SnapshotRegistry data structures, the per-domain control managers (replication, cluster, feature, producer-id, config, etc.), broker liveness and fencing, leader/ISR recomputation via PartitionChangeBuilder, replica placement, and periodic tasks, with failover semantics and KRaft-vs-ZK contrast.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The event loop and the asynchronous API
Timeline data structures: reversible in-memory state
Becoming (and ceasing to be) the active controller
keyFacts: The QuorumController is single-threaded: one KafkaEventQueue thread named 'quorum-controller-{nodeId}-event-handler' processes all events, eliminating locks on controller state (QuorumController.java:164,406; KafkaEventQueue.java:43).
A node is the active controller iff curClaimEpoch != -1 (a volatile int, written only on the controller thread, read by RPC threads); -1 means standby (QuorumController.java:1159,1483).

Write→commit→apply: ControllerWriteEvent.run() generates records, calls raftClient.prepareAppend(epoch,records), immediately replay()s each record to update in-memory state, then schedulePreparedAppend(); the client future completes only after the record's offset is committed/stable (QuorumController.java:791-863).

The active controller applies records optimistically before they commit; on commit it only advances offsets and drains the purgatory, while standby controllers replay the committed records themselves in handleCommit (QuorumController.java:991-1023).

Determinism guarantee: every controller in the quorum reaches byte-identical in-memory state by replaying the same committed metadata records in offset order via the single replay() dispatch switch (QuorumController.java:1238); failover transfers no state.

Timeline collections (TimelineHashMap/Set/Long/Object) backed by SnapshotRegistry implement copy-on-write tiers keyed by metadata offset; revertToSnapshot(lastStableOffset) discards uncommitted records on renounce, and metadata-transaction aborts revert to transactionStartOffset-1 (SnapshottableHashTable.java:89,413; SnapshotRegistry.java:253; OffsetControlManager.java:382).

OffsetControlManager tracks three offsets — nextWriteOffset, lastCommittedOffset (Raft HWM), and lastStableOffset; reads use lastStableOffset, which is clamped to min(transactionStartOffset-1, lastCommittedOffset) so partial transactions are never visible (OffsetControlManager.java:118,319).

Broker heartbeats are deliberately NOT written to the metadata log; BrokerHeartbeatManager/Tracker keep last-contact time and reported offset as soft state only on the active controller, rebuilt after failover, trading a small metadata log for extra post-failover fencing latency (BrokerHeartbeatManager.java:44,51).

Heartbeat contact times are updated lock-free from RPC threads via a ConcurrentHashMap in BrokerHeartbeatTracker, so liveness survives a backlogged controller thread; timed-out heartbeat events still refresh liveness via processExpiredBrokerHeartbeat (QuorumController.java:1978; BrokerHeartbeatTracker.java:32,131; ReplicationControlManager.java:1718).

The default broker session timeout is 9 s; maybeFenceStaleBroker samples ~8x per timeout (period = sessionTimeout/8) and fences at most one broker per run, so a dead broker is fenced within ~112.5% of the session timeout (ClusterControlManager.java:84; QuorumController.java:1684,1696).

A fenced broker is unfenced only once its reported currentMetadataOffset >= the offset of its own RegisterBrokerRecord; controlled-shutdown completes only when controlledShutdownOffset <= the lowest active broker offset and the broker leads no partitions (BrokerHeartbeatManager.java:415,456).

PartitionChangeBuilder recomputes leader/ISR/ELR for one partition and emits an Optional<PartitionChangeRecord>; its Election enum (PREFERRED/ONLINE/UNCLEAN) selects policy, and broker fence/unfence/shutdown fan out through generateLeaderAndIsrUpdates (PartitionChangeBuilder.java:68,106; ReplicationControlManager.java:1399,2051).

FeatureControlManager gates the controller on metadata.version (a TimelineObject) per KIP-584; updateFeatures refuses a level unless the controller software, every registered broker, AND every controller support it, and refuses if any controller has not yet registered (FeatureControlManager.java:147,321; QuorumController.java:395).

Records are written atomically (single Raft batch, all-or-nothing) when ControllerResult.isAtomic(); both atomic batches and any single user operation are capped at 10000 records (DEFAULT_MAX_RECORDS_PER_BATCH / MAX_RECORDS_PER_USER_OP) (QuorumController.java:180,187,898).

Leadership transitions run on the Raft listener thread: claim() sets curClaimEpoch, activates managers, and prepends a completeActivation event (running ActivationRecordsGenerator, which finishes/aborts any dangling metadata transaction); renounce() resigns, fails the purgatory with NotControllerException, and reverts state to lastStableOffset (QuorumController.java:1107,1163,1213; ActivationRecordsGenerator.java:40).

KRaft replaces the ZooKeeper-backed controller (KIP-500/KIP-631); ZooKeeper was fully removed in 4.0, leaving only ZkMigrationStateRecord as a replay no-op; ELR/strict-min-ISR logic is KIP-966 and metadata transactions are KIP-868 (QuorumController.java:1311; PartitionChangeBuilder.java; OffsetControlManager.java:382).

Default replication factor is 3 and default partitions is 1; StripedReplicaPlacer spreads replicas across racks (top priority) then brokers, prefers unfenced brokers, and enforces one-replica-per-broker plus an unfenced leader (QuorumController.java:203-205; StripedReplicaPlacer.java:33).

Periodic tasks (only on the active controller) include maybeFenceStaleBroker, electPreferred (opt-in), electUnclean (5 min), writeNoOpRecord (opt-in liveness), expireDelegationTokens (5 min); both election tasks elect at most MAX_ELECTIONS_PER_IMBALANCE=1000 per run (QuorumController.java:1667-1760; ReplicationControlManager.java:151,1785).

title: Metadata Propagation & Broker Lifecycle
abstract: Traces how committed metadata records in the single-partition __cluster_metadata log become materialized broker state, and how brokers join and leave the cluster. Covers the MetadataLoader consuming RaftClient commits/snapshots on a single thread, folding them into immutable MetadataImages via incremental MetadataDeltas (including KIP-866 transactions), and publishing each new image to an ordered chain of MetadataPublishers; the broker-side BrokerMetadataPublisher applying topic deltas to ReplicaManager and the coordinators and updating KRaftMetadataCache; metadata.version/feature-level gating (KIP-584); and the BrokerLifecycleManager + AssignmentsManager registration/heartbeat/fencing state machine (KIP-631, KIP-841).
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: The metadata log is the single-partition internal topic __cluster_metadata (partition 0); the constant is Topic.CLUSTER_METADATA_TOPIC_NAME and KafkaRaftServer aliases it as MetadataPartition with MetadataTopicId = Uuid.METADATA_TOPIC_ID (clients/.../internals/Topic.java:30, core/.../KafkaRaftServer.scala:118).

MetadataImage is an immutable Java record of 10 components (provenance, FeaturesImage, ClusterImage, TopicsImage, ConfigurationsImage, ClientQuotasImage, ProducerIdsImage, AclsImage, ScramImage, DelegationTokenImage); MetadataDelta.apply(provenance) builds the next image, reusing unchanged sub-images by reference (metadata/.../image/MetadataImage.java:31, MetadataDelta.java:387).

MetadataLoader implements RaftClient.Listener and runs all handleCommit/handleLoadSnapshot/handleLeaderChange callbacks serialized on a single KafkaEventQueue thread named 'kafka-N-metadata-loader-'; it is registered via client.register(loader) (metadata/.../loader/MetadataLoader.java:396; core/.../SharedServer.scala:358).

The loader will not publish to broker subsystems until caught up: catchingUp stays true until the loaded offset reaches highWaterMark-1 and at least one controller record has been seen (MetadataLoader.java:249).

MetadataBatchLoader folds multiple Raft batches into one MetadataDelta and implements KIP-866 metadata transactions via a TransactionState machine (BEGIN/END/ABORT records); an aborted transaction publishes an empty delta on the pre-transaction image (metadata/.../loader/MetadataBatchLoader.java:190).

KRaftMetadataCache stores one 'volatile MetadataImage currentImage' replaced wholesale by setImage(); readers are lock-free and must read the reference once for snapshot consistency; fenced brokers are filtered out of Metadata responses in maybeFilterAliveReplicas (metadata/.../KRaftMetadataCache.java:74, :92, :485).

BrokerMetadataPublisher.onMetadataUpdate sets the cache image, on first publish starts LogManager/ReplicaManager/coordinators, calls replicaManager.applyDelta(topicsDelta,newImage), drives coordinator election/resignation, and completes firstPublishFuture in a finally block (core/.../metadata/BrokerMetadataPublisher.scala:111).

TopicDelta.localChanges(brokerId) classifies each changed partition into deletes/leaders/electedLeaders/followers using partitionEpoch and leaderEpoch bumps (electedLeaders is the subset of leaders whose leaderEpoch also changed) (metadata/.../image/TopicDelta.java:176).

metadata.version is a finalized feature level (KIP-584) stored as a FeatureLevelRecord; MINIMUM_VERSION is IBP_3_3_IV3 (level 7), LATEST_PRODUCTION is IBP_4_3_IV0 (level 30), and IBP_4_4_IV0 (level 31, share-group DLQ KIP-1191) is unstable; a metadata.version change triggers handleMetadataVersionChange on every sub-delta (server-common/.../MetadataVersion.java:136/:157, image/MetadataDelta.java:328).

BrokerLifecycleManager runs the broker state machine NOT_RUNNING→STARTING→RECOVERY→RUNNING→PENDING_CONTROLLED_SHUTDOWN→SHUTTING_DOWN on a 'broker-N-lifecycle-manager-' event-queue thread; state and brokerEpoch are volatile but written only on that thread (server/.../BrokerLifecycleManager.java:125, :645).

Broker registration sends incarnationId (a random per-process Uuid), listeners, supported feature ranges, sorted log-dir UUIDs and previousBrokerEpoch; the controller returns the brokerEpoch lease; failing to register within initial.broker.registration.timeout.ms (default 60000) shuts the broker down (BrokerLifecycleManager.java:103/:475/:732).

Brokers self-fence: heartbeats set wantFence=!readyToUnfence and currentMetadataOffset=loader.lastAppliedOffset(); the controller's response booleans isCaughtUp/isFenced/shouldShutDown drive transitions; heartbeat interval is broker.heartbeat.interval.ms (default 2000), and the controller-side lease is broker.session.timeout.ms (default 9000) (BrokerLifecycleManager.java:564; raft/.../KRaftConfigs.java:39/:43).

BrokerRegistrationTracker is a publisher that resends the broker registration if the cluster MV reaches JBOD support (>=3.7-IV2) but the broker's registration still lists no directories, via lifecycleManager.resendBrokerRegistration() (metadata/.../publisher/BrokerRegistrationTracker.java:67; core/.../BrokerServer.scala:582).

AssignmentsManager batches JBOD directory-placement intents (AssignReplicasToDirs RPC) on a dedicated event-queue thread, draining up to MAX_ASSIGNMENTS_PER_REQUEST from a ConcurrentHashMap 'ready', validating each against the current image, and retrying failures with exponential backoff (100ms base, x2, 10s cap) (server/.../AssignmentsManager.java:62/:329).

BrokerServer startup blocks on three futures in order before serving: installPublishers, lifecycleManager.initialCatchUpFuture (controller acknowledges caught-up), and the first metadata publish; loading a snapshot calls delta.finishSnapshot() so deletions in the old image are honored on catch-up (core/.../BrokerServer.scala:594; image/MetadataDelta.java:375).

PartitionRegistration exposes public-final fields replicas, directories (parallel JBOD dir UUIDs), isr, elr/lastKnownElr (KIP-966), leader, leaderRecoveryState, leaderEpoch and partitionEpoch; merge(PartitionChangeRecord) treats null record arrays as 'unchanged' (metadata/.../metadata/PartitionRegistration.java:153/:238).

title: Group Coordination & Rebalance Protocols
abstract: The modern Java group coordinator runs as a replicated state machine: each __consumer_offsets partition is one coordinator shard (GroupCoordinatorShard) whose durable state is the partition log itself — group/offset mutations are written as records and replayed to rebuild in-memory state, with responses gated on replication past the high watermark. The chapter covers the CoordinatorRuntime write/read/commit lifecycle, the GroupMetadataManager + OffsetMetadataManager managers and their record schemas, the legacy classic protocol (JoinGroup/SyncGroup, generations, eager vs cooperative rebalancing) and the KIP-848 consumer protocol (incremental ConsumerGroupHeartbeat, server-side assignors, epoch-based reconciliation via CurrentAssignmentBuilder), plus offset management, static membership, threading, failure/recovery, and invariants.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The runtime: a partition as a replicated state machine
keyFacts: A group is served by exactly one coordinator: the leader of __consumer_offsets partition p = abs(groupId.hashCode()) % numPartitions, computed in GroupCoordinatorService.partitionFor (GroupCoordinatorService.java:424). The topic name __consumer_offsets is Topic.GROUP_METADATA_TOPIC_NAME.

The coordinator's durable state IS the __consumer_offsets log: every mutation is a CoordinatorRecord; in-memory state is a deterministic replay of committed records via GroupCoordinatorShard.replay (GroupCoordinatorShard.java:1163), used identically during loading and request handling. Failover = transition LOADING and replay the partition.

Write lifecycle (CoordinatorRuntime): a handler returns CoordinatorResult{records, response}; records are applied via replay and appended to the log (lastWrittenOffset advances), but the client response is deferred until the partition high watermark reaches that offset (HighWatermarkListener -> deferredEventQueue.completeUpTo, CoordinatorRuntime.java:1830). Acknowledged results therefore survive coordinator failover.

KIP-848 consumer protocol uses a single repeated ConsumerGroupHeartbeat RPC with three epochs: group epoch (bumped on membership/subscription/metadata-hash change), target-assignment epoch (bumped when the server assignor runs), and per-member epoch. There is no leader, no client-side assignor, and no global synchronization barrier; GA in Apache Kafka 4.0.

The heartbeat handler runs a 3-step pipeline (GroupMetadataManager.java:2441): (1) create/update member and maybe bump group epoch via updateSubscriptionMetadata (:3982), (2) maybe recompute target assignment with the server assignor (maybeUpdateTargetAssignment :4093, throttled by group.consumer.assignment.interval.ms default 1000ms), (3) reconcile the member via maybeReconcile (:3796).

CurrentAssignmentBuilder (CurrentAssignmentBuilder.java) is the KIP-848 reconciliation state machine over MemberState {STABLE=0, UNREVOKED_PARTITIONS=1, UNRELEASED_PARTITIONS=2, UNKNOWN=127}. It enforces revoke-before-assign (a member stays on its current epoch in UNREVOKED_PARTITIONS until it reports the partitions are no longer owned) and no-double-ownership (a target partition with currentPartitionEpoch != -1 is held in UNRELEASED_PARTITIONS).

Server-side assignors are configured via group.consumer.assignors (default list [uniform, range]; first is default). UniformAssignor (name 'uniform') balances evenly with homogeneous/heterogeneous builders; RangeAssignor (name 'range') assigns contiguous co-partitioned ranges. An unknown serverAssignor is rejected with UnsupportedAssignorException (GroupCoordinatorService.java:473).

The classic protocol is a five-state machine EMPTY/PREPARING_REBALANCE/COMPLETING_REBALANCE/STABLE/DEAD (ClassicGroupState.java), client-coordinated: the first member to join becomes leader and computes the assignment locally; protocol is chosen by member vote (ClassicGroup.selectProtocol). Eager assignors (RangeAssignor) revoke all partitions each rebalance; CooperativeStickyAssignor (KIP-429) supports [COOPERATIVE, EAGER] and revokes incrementally.

Offsets live in a three-level timeline map group->topic->partition->OffsetAndMetadata (OffsetMetadataManager Offsets, OffsetMetadataManager.java:356). OffsetCommit (apiKey 1) value carries offset, leaderEpoch (v3+), metadata, commitTimestamp, and tagged topicId (v4+); a null value is a tombstone. Metadata over offset.metadata.max.bytes (default 4096) is rejected with OFFSET_METADATA_TOO_LARGE.

Offset retention: a periodic sweep (offsets.retention.check.interval.ms default 600000ms) expires offsets only for topics the group is no longer subscribed to and older than offsets.retention.minutes (default 10080 = 7 days) with no pending transactional offset (cleanupExpiredOffsets, OffsetMetadataManager.java:1046); fully-expired empty groups are deleted.

Liveness: per-member session timer keyed by group+memberId (group.consumer.session.timeout.ms default 45000, bounds 45000/60000); heartbeat interval advertised via group.consumer.heartbeat.interval.ms (default 5000, bounds 5000/15000). A rebalance timer is armed only while a member is in UNREVOKED_PARTITIONS. Members leave by heartbeating with epoch -1 (LEAVE_GROUP_MEMBER_EPOCH) or -2 (static).

Threading: GroupCoordinatorService is a stateless front door; a MultiThreadedEventProcessor with group.coordinator.threads (default 4) processes per-partition event queues single-threaded-and-ordered (so handlers need no locks). A CoordinatorBackgroundThreadPoolExecutor (group.coordinator.background.threads default 2) runs regex resolution and offloaded assignment (group.consumer.assignor.offload.enable default true). All hard state is in SnapshotRegistry-backed timeline structures.

Consumer-group state is spread across many small records: ConsumerGroupMetadataValue (Epoch + 64-bit MetadataHash, replacing the legacy per-group subscription-metadata record apiKey 4), ConsumerGroupMemberMetadataValue (apiKey 5), ConsumerGroupTargetAssignmentMetadata/Member (apiKey 6/7), and ConsumerGroupCurrentMemberAssignmentValue (apiKey 8) which carries MemberEpoch, PreviousMemberEpoch, State, AssignedPartitions, PartitionsPendingRevocation, and a tagged AssignmentEpochs[] used to fence zombie commits.

Static membership (KIP-345) maps group.instance.id -> memberId (ConsumerGroup.staticMembers) so a restarting instance reclaims its assignment without a full rebalance; in the classic protocol it lives in GroupMetadataValue.MemberMetadata.groupInstanceId. The same runtime and shard also back Share Groups (KIP-932) and Streams groups (KIP-1071).

Recovery safety: if partitionWriter.append returns an unexpected offset the runtime force-reloads the partition (FAILED then LOADING, CoordinatorRuntime.java:721); while loading, RPCs get COORDINATOR_LOAD_IN_PROGRESS / COORDINATOR_NOT_AVAILABLE; wrong-coordinator requests get NOT_COORDINATOR. A lost epoch-bump is recovered via PreviousMemberEpoch; a stale member epoch on commit yields STALE_MEMBER_EPOCH (KIP-1319, v6+) or legacy ILLEGAL_GENERATION.

title: Transactions & Exactly-Once Semantics
abstract: A reference chapter on Kafka's two-layer exactly-once stack: the idempotent producer (PID + epoch + per-partition sequence, deduped and validated by the leader's ProducerStateManager/ProducerAppendInfo) and transactions (KIP-98), in which a TransactionCoordinator maps a transactional.id to a PID with epoch fencing, drives a two-phase commit over the internal __transaction_state log, and writes COMMIT/ABORT transaction markers (control records) to every involved partition via TransactionMarkerChannelManager. It covers the TransactionMetadata state machine, on-disk record/byte layouts, the consumer-side LSO + aborted-transaction index for read_committed, the concurrency/locking model, configuration, failure/recovery, and the TV_2 (KIP-890) per-transaction epoch bump.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The protocol flow, step by step
The consumer side: read_committed, LSO & the aborted index
keyFacts: Producer IDs (PIDs) are allocated by the active KRaft controller in fixed blocks of 1000 (ProducerIdsBlock.PRODUCER_ID_BLOCK_SIZE=1000); the controller emits a monotonic ProducerIdsRecord and asserts strict monotonicity on replay so a PID is never reused (metadata/.../ProducerIdControlManager.java:84-120).

On the broker, RPCProducerIdManager serves PIDs lock-free via an AtomicLong getAndIncrement on the current block and prefetches the next block at 90% consumption (PID_PREFETCH_THRESHOLD=0.90); when exhausted it throws the retriable COORDINATOR_LOAD_IN_PROGRESS rather than REQUEST_TIMED_OUT (transaction-coordinator/.../RPCProducerIdManager.java:80-109).

The partition leader deduplicates producer retries using ProducerStateEntry, which retains the last 5 batches (NUM_BATCHES_TO_RETAIN=5); an exact (baseSequence,lastSequence) match for the current epoch returns the original offset/timestamp and skips the append (storage/.../ProducerStateEntry.java:35,128-137; UnifiedLog.java:1247-1254).

Idempotent sequence validation requires appendFirstSeq == currentLastSeq+1 (wrapping Integer.MAX_VALUE to 0) for the same epoch and sequence 0 to start a new epoch; violations throw OutOfOrderSequenceException, which is why the idempotent producer caps in-flight requests at 5 (storage/.../ProducerAppendInfo.java:156-198).

Each transactional.id maps deterministically to one __transaction_state partition via abs(transactionalId.hashCode) % transactionTopicPartitionCount (default 50 partitions), fixing which broker is its coordinator; changing that count after deploy breaks fencing and the loader throws KafkaException (TransactionStateManager.scala:439,641-646).
