TransactionMetadata is a state machine over 8 states (EMPTY, ONGOING, PREPARE_COMMIT, PREPARE_ABORT, COMPLETE_COMMIT, COMPLETE_ABORT, PREPARE_EPOCH_FENCE, DEAD) with an enforced VALID_PREVIOUS_STATES map; every transition is two-phase: prepareXxx sets pendingState, completeTransitionTo applies it only after the log write succeeds (TransactionState.java:31-103; TransactionMetadata.java:318-446).

Transaction state lives in the compacted internal topic __transaction_state (default 50 partitions, RF 3, min.insync.replicas=2, uncompressed, unclean.leader.election=false); the TransactionLogValue record holds producerId, prev/next producer ids, epoch, timeout, status, partition list, timestamps and clientTransactionVersion (TransactionCoordinator.scala:1014-1022; TransactionLogValue.json).

EndTxn is a two-phase commit: durably append PREPARE_COMMIT/PREPARE_ABORT (the commit point), respond to the client early, then TransactionMarkerChannelManager writes COMMIT/ABORT markers to every involved partition leader, and only after all are acknowledged append COMPLETE_COMMIT/COMPLETE_ABORT (TransactionCoordinator.scala:758-1007; TransactionMarkerChannelManager.scala:276-383).

A transaction marker is a control record (EndTransactionMarker): key=ControlRecordType (0=ABORT,1=COMMIT), value=EndTxnMarker carrying the coordinator epoch; receiving leaders reject markers with an older coordinator epoch (TransactionCoordinatorFencedException) and, under TV_2, markers whose epoch is not strictly greater than current (EndTransactionMarker.java; ProducerAppendInfo.java:117-153,246-257).

Zombie fencing uses the producer epoch: InitProducerId bumps the epoch and aborts any dangling ONGOING transaction by routing through PREPARE_EPOCH_FENCE then returning CONCURRENT_TRANSACTIONS; a stale-epoch client write gets the recoverable InvalidProducerEpochException, not the legacy fatal ProducerFenced (TransactionCoordinator.scala:228-296; ProducerAppendInfo.java:145-152).

read_committed correctness rests on the Last Stable Offset = min(highWatermark, firstUnstableOffset); consumers never read past the LSO and client-side CompletedFetch skips every batch whose producerId is in an aborted transaction recorded in the per-segment TransactionIndex (UnifiedLog.java:678-686; ProducerStateManager.java:246-258; CompletedFetch.java:187-244).

An aborted-transaction index entry (AbortedTxn) is a fixed 32-byte record of four int64s: ProducerId, FirstOffset, LastOffset, LastStableOffset; it is returned alongside read_committed fetch responses so consumers can filter aborted data (storage/.../TransactionIndex.java; AbortedTxn.json).

KIP-447 sendOffsetsToTransaction binds consumer offsets into the transaction by passing full ConsumerGroupMetadata (group id + generation + member id) so the group coordinator fences offset commits by generation; offsets land in __consumer_offsets but stay LSO-gated until the COMMIT marker is written there (clients/.../TransactionManager.java:432-463).

TV_2 (KIP-890, transaction.version feature, LATEST_PRODUCTION=TV_2) bumps the producer epoch on every commit/abort so each transaction is uniquely identified by (pid,epoch); EndTxnResponse returns the new pid/epoch, clients skip InitProducerId after startup, and the PID rotates to nextProducerId on epoch overflow (TransactionVersion.java:24-100; TransactionMetadata.java:265-288).

Coordinator failover replays the compacted __transaction_state partition and, for any transaction loaded in PREPARE_COMMIT/PREPARE_ABORT, immediately re-drives phase 2 (re-sends markers, writes COMPLETE_*), making the prepare record the durable commit point that survives a coordinator crash (TransactionStateManager.scala:538-597).

Background scheduler tasks: transaction-abort aborts timed-out ONGOING transactions every 10s; transactionalId-expiration tombstones idle ids every 1h (transactional.id.expiration.ms default 7 days; transaction.max.timeout.ms default 15 min); distributed 2PC transactions with txnTimeoutMs==Integer.MAX_VALUE are exempt from timeout (TransactionCoordinator.scala:1047-1095; TransactionStateManagerConfig.java).

title: Share Groups (Queues / KIP-932)
abstract: Share groups are Kafka's queue-like consumption model (KIP-932): many share consumers cooperatively read the same partitions, and records are leased individually under a time-bounded acquisition lock instead of being committed by offset. Each record has a delivery state (Available, Acquired, Acknowledged, Archiving, Archived) and a delivery count, giving at-least-once semantics with bounded redelivery and an optional dead-letter queue. The broker's SharePartition tracks in-flight state and the share-partition start offset; durable state is persisted to the internal __share_group_state topic via a persister SPI to the ShareCoordinator. This chapter follows the path from the ShareConsumer client through ShareFetch/ShareAcknowledge RPCs into SharePartition and the coordinator, including concurrency, persistence, DLQ, and a comparison with consumer groups and transactions.
sections: Where it lives in the code
Share groups vs. consumer groups vs. transactions
keyFacts: A share group lets N share consumers cooperatively consume the SAME topic-partition (N:M), unlike consumer groups where each partition is assigned to exactly one member; this decouples parallelism from partition count (KIP-932).

Records have a per-record RecordState with byte ids: AVAILABLE(0), ACQUIRED(1), ACKNOWLEDGED(2), ARCHIVING(3, KIP-1191), ARCHIVED(4); ACKNOWLEDGED and ARCHIVED are terminal (server/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/server/share/fetch/RecordState.java:26).
Acknowledgement types map to states: ACCEPT→ACKNOWLEDGED, RELEASE→AVAILABLE, REJECT→ARCHIVED, and a gap(byte 0)→ARCHIVED (core/src/main/java/kafka/server/share/SharePartition.java:149).

Each acquire leases records under a time-bounded acquisition lock implemented as an AcquisitionLockTimerTask; on expiry records return to AVAILABLE, or are archived/DLQ'd if delivery count is exhausted (server/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/server/share/fetch/AcquisitionLockTimerTask.java:67; SharePartition.java:2961).

The broker SharePartition holds in-flight state in cachedState, a ConcurrentSkipListMap keyed by each in-flight batch's first offset, guarded by a ReentrantReadWriteLock; it tracks startOffset (SPSO) and endOffset (SharePartition.java:178,276,282,393).

The share-partition start offset (SPSO) only advances over a contiguous prefix of terminal records via maybeUpdateCachedStateAndOffsets; a single stuck record at the head pins the SPSO and blocks log aging (SharePartition.java:2643,2728).

Default config: group.share.delivery.count.limit=5 (range 2-10), group.share.record.lock.duration.ms=30000, group.share.partition.max.record.locks=2000 (max in-flight records) (group-coordinator/.../share/ShareGroupConfig.java:49,61,37).

Acknowledgement uses persist-before-commit: state transitions are staged with a rollback snapshot, writeShareGroupState RPC persists to the coordinator, and only on success is the in-memory state committed; on failure it rolls back to ACQUIRED (or AVAILABLE if the lease lapsed) (SharePartition.java:2564,2858; InFlightState.java:203,219).

Durable state lives in the internal compacted topic __share_group_state (default 50 partitions, RF 3, min ISR 2) as two coordinator record types: full ShareSnapshotValue and incremental ShareUpdateValue, each containing run-length StateBatch entries (firstOffset,lastOffset,deliveryState int8,deliveryCount int16) (share-coordinator/.../ShareCoordinatorConfig.java:38,42,47; ShareSnapshotValue.json:38).

Share-partition leaders write state through the persister SPI (DefaultStatePersister, config group.share.persister.class.name) which uses PersisterStateManager to FindCoordinator and issue WriteShareGroupState/ReadShareGroupState RPCs; the owning coordinator is Utils.abs(key.asCoordinatorKey().hashCode()) % numPartitions (server-common/.../persister/DefaultStatePersister.java:46; ShareCoordinatorService.java:284).

The ShareCoordinator writes a new snapshot every share.coordinator.snapshot.update.records.per.snapshot updates (default 500), else an incremental update; overlapping batches are merged by PersisterStateBatchCombiner (ShareCoordinatorShard.java:648,662,729; ShareCoordinatorConfig.java:58).

Dead-letter queue (KIP-1191): records exhausting the delivery limit pass through ARCHIVING and are produced to a DLQ topic by ShareGroupDLQStateManager (own InterBrokerSendThread, coalesced ProduceRequests with acks=-1, max 5 retries), tagged with __dlq.errors.* headers; only after a successful DLQ produce does the record become ARCHIVED (server/.../dlq/ShareGroupDLQStateManager.java:88,242,922).

DLQ is configured per group via errors.deadletterqueue.topic.name (default empty = disabled) and errors.deadletterqueue.copy.record.enable (default false, copies original key/value vs headers only) (group-coordinator/.../GroupConfig.java:113,117).

The client ShareConsumerImpl.poll piggybacks acknowledgements on the next ShareFetch; share.acknowledgement.mode is implicit (poll auto-ACCEPTs the prior batch) or explicit (app must acknowledge each record); share.acquire.mode defaults to batch_optimized vs record_limit (ShareConsumerImpl.java:597; ShareAcknowledgementMode.java:29; ConsumerConfig.java:375,386,391).

Share groups give at-least-once with no cross-consumer ordering and use __share_group_state, in contrast to consumer groups (one committed offset per partition, __consumer_offsets) and transactions/EOS (exactly-once, __transaction_state); KIP-1226 added DeliveryCompleteCount to persisted state for share-partition lag.

ShareFetch/ShareAcknowledge/ShareGroupHeartbeat RPCs are routed in KafkaApis; ShareFetchResponse returns AcquiredRecords ranges (FirstOffset, LastOffset, DeliveryCount int16) plus a per-response AcquisitionLockTimeoutMs (core/src/main/scala/kafka/server/KafkaApis.scala:238,240,241; clients/.../message/ShareFetchResponse.json).

title: The Producer Client
abstract: A deep, code-grounded reference on the Kafka 4.4 KafkaProducer, tracing a record from send() to broker ack: serialization and interceptors, partition selection (explicit / key-hash / adaptive sticky BuiltInPartitioner), append into the RecordAccumulator's per-partition deque of ProducerBatch objects backed by the bounded BufferPool, and the single Sender I/O thread that drains ready batches, builds ProduceRequests, pipelines them through the NetworkClient, and dispatches retries, backoff, and completion callbacks. Covers idempotence (producer id/epoch/sequence), acks/timeout/retry semantics, batch-level compression, the threading/locking model, error classification, flush()/close(), failure modes, and invariants.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The send pipeline, end to end
ProducerBatch: structure, futures, and the state machine
The Sender thread: drain, encode, transmit, complete
keyFacts: KafkaProducer.send() is asynchronous and never performs network I/O on the calling thread; it runs interceptors, waits on metadata, serializes key/value, selects a partition, and appends bytes into the RecordAccumulator, returning a Future<RecordMetadata> immediately (KafkaProducer.java:1092, 1125).

A single background I/O thread named 'kafka-producer-network-thread | <clientId>' runs the Sender Runnable and owns all socket interaction, retries, and the in-flight pipeline (KafkaProducer.java:251, 475; Sender.java:240).

Partition selection priority: explicit record.partition() > custom partitioner.class > murmur2 key hash modulo partition count > UNKNOWN_PARTITION deferred to the adaptive BuiltInPartitioner inside the accumulator (KafkaProducer.java:1625; BuiltInPartitioner.java:397).

The built-in partitioner (one per topic) is an adaptive uniform sticky partitioner (KIP-794, evolving KIP-480); it rotates the sticky partition after producedBytes >= batch.size (and unconditionally at 2x batch.size), and with adaptive partitioning enabled (default true) weights keyless partition choice inversely by per-partition queue depth via a cumulative frequency table (BuiltInPartitioner.java:251, 270, 111).

The RecordAccumulator stores batches as ConcurrentMap<topic,TopicInfo> each holding ConcurrentMap<partition, ArrayDeque<ProducerBatch>>; appends and the Sender's ready/drain both synchronize on the individual partition deque monitor (RecordAccumulator.java:1301, 319).

The BufferPool bounds memory at buffer.memory (default 32 MiB), recycles buffers of exactly batch.size on a free list, and blocks over-budget allocations up to the remaining max.block.ms on a FIFO waiter queue before throwing BufferExhaustedException (BufferPool.java:45, 159).

enable.idempotence defaults to true and requires acks=all and max.in.flight.requests.per.connection <= 5 (hard-capped, ConfigException otherwise); idempotence stamps each batch with (producerId, epoch, baseSequence) at drain time so the broker deduplicates retries and rejects out-of-order sequences (ProducerConfig.java:541, 639; RecordAccumulator.java:911).

Ordering holds for any max.in.flight in [1,5] when idempotence is on because the broker enforces strictly increasing per-partition sequences; without idempotence only max.in.flight=1 preserves order, enforced by partition muting during in-flight sends (Sender.java:420; RecordAccumulator.java:823).
acks=0 yields offset -1 and no response; acks=1 is leader-only; acks=all (default) waits for the in-sync replica set; only acks=all is compatible with idempotence (ProducerConfig.java:403).

Three composing timeouts: request.timeout.ms (30s) bounds one round trip, retries (default Integer.MAX_VALUE) bounds attempts, and delivery.timeout.ms (120s) is the hard wall-clock deadline >= linger.ms + request.timeout.ms; in practice delivery.timeout.ms terminates retrying (KafkaProducer.java:589; ProducerConfig.java:419).

Error handling in Sender.completeBatch: MESSAGE_TOO_LARGE on a multi-record batch triggers split-and-reenqueue without charging a retry; retriable errors reenqueue with exponential backoff; DUPLICATE_SEQUENCE_NUMBER completes as success with unknown offset; auth and version errors are fatal (Sender.java:671, 876).

Compression is set once via compression.type (default none; gzip/snappy/lz4/zstd) and applied at the batch level by MemoryRecordsBuilder, so larger batches compress better; a per-(topic,codec) CompressionRatioEstimator sizes buffers (KafkaProducer.java:562; ProducerBatch.java:518).

ProducerBatch has a CAS-guarded final-state machine (SUCCEEDED/FAILED/ABORTED) tolerating double completion (an expired in-flight batch may later succeed on the broker); a SUCCEEDED batch may never re-transition (ProducerBatch.java:262).

flush() sets flushesInProgress so ready() returns all batches, wakes the Sender, and awaits each incomplete ProduceRequestResult including split-batch dependents; calling flush() or a blocking close() from inside a callback is rejected/degraded because the callback runs on the I/O thread (KafkaProducer.java:1366, 1554; RecordAccumulator.java:1106).

The NetworkClient bounds pipelining via InFlightRequests.canSendMore: a node may receive another request only when its oldest send has completed and its in-flight queue size is below max.in.flight.requests.per.connection (NetworkClient.java:544; InFlightRequests.java:96).

On an OutOfOrderSequenceException an idempotent (non-transactional) producer bumps its epoch locally (or resets to a new producer id at Short.MAX_VALUE) and rewrites in-flight sequences from 0; expired in-flight batches mark the partition sequence unresolved to halt new draining until resolved (TransactionManager.java:827, 673; Sender.java:373).

title: The Consumer Client
abstract: A source-grounded reference on the Kafka consumer client. KafkaConsumer is a façade delegating (via group.protocol) to either the legacy single-threaded ClassicKafkaConsumer or the KIP-848 AsyncKafkaConsumer, which splits work between the application thread and a ConsumerNetworkThread over ApplicationEvent/BackgroundEvent queues driven by RequestManagers. Covers SubscriptionState and its fetch-state machine, the fetch pipeline (request building, decode, max.poll.records, read_committed aborted-batch skipping), offset management (auto/manual commit, committed-offset fetch, auto.offset.reset), and the async member state machine with poll-timer liveness.
sections: Where it lives in the code
The classic engine: one thread does everything
The async engine: two threads, two queues
keyFacts: KafkaConsumer is a thin façade holding a single ConsumerDelegate<K,V> field and forwarding every call; ConsumerDelegateCreator.create() reads group.protocol and returns AsyncKafkaConsumer for value 'consumer' (KIP-848) or ClassicKafkaConsumer otherwise (ConsumerDelegateCreator.java:61). group.protocol defaults to 'classic' (ConsumerConfig.java:115).

The async engine (AsyncKafkaConsumer) runs a background daemon thread named 'consumer_background_thread' (ConsumerNetworkThread.java:68) whose runOnce() loop drains the application-event queue, calls RequestManager.poll(now) on each manager, then networkClientDelegate.poll(); the application thread never performs network I/O.

Async poll() submits a non-blocking multi-stage AsyncPollEvent (reconciliation check → updateTimerAndMaybeCommit/heartbeat poll-timer reset → updateFetchPositions → createFetchRequests) processed on the background thread, while the app thread polls the FetchBuffer; volatile flags isValidatePositionsComplete and isReconciliationCheckComplete gate when the app thread may collect records (events/ApplicationEventProcessor.java:757, events/AsyncPollEvent.java).

The classic engine runs the entire coordinator+fetch loop on the user's poll() thread: coordinator.poll(timer) does JoinGroup/SyncGroup/rebalance callbacks/auto-commit, then fetcher.collectFetch()/sendFetches()/client.poll() (ClassicKafkaConsumer.java:648); only heartbeats use a separate thread named 'kafka-coordinator-heartbeat-thread | <groupId>' (AbstractCoordinator.java:124).

The consumer is not thread-safe; both engines enforce single-thread access with an AtomicLong currentThread + AtomicInteger refCount CAS lock that throws ConcurrentModificationException instead of blocking (AsyncKafkaConsumer.java:2216). wakeup() is the only safe cross-thread call.

SubscriptionState (all methods synchronized) tracks a single subscription type — NONE, AUTO_TOPICS, AUTO_PATTERN, AUTO_PATTERN_RE2J, USER_ASSIGNED, AUTO_TOPICS_SHARE — enforcing that subscribe() and assign() are mutually exclusive (SubscriptionState.java:80,185). Each partition has a FetchState (INITIALIZING/FETCHING/AWAIT_RESET/AWAIT_VALIDATION) with an explicit transition table (SubscriptionState.java:1306).

Per-partition TopicPartitionState holds the FetchPosition (offset of next record + offsetEpoch + currentLeader), highWatermark, logStartOffset, lastStableOffset, paused, pendingRevocation, resetStrategy, and a leased preferredReadReplica; a partition is fetchable only when !paused && !pendingRevocation && !pendingOnAssignedCallback && hasValidPosition() (SubscriptionState.java:1011,1250).

Fetch requests are built per leader node with request-level fetch.max.wait.ms/fetch.min.bytes/fetch.max.bytes and per-partition max.partition.fetch.bytes as FetchConfig.fetchSize (AbstractFetch.java:310,421); a node already hosting buffered partitions is skipped to preserve the broker fetch session. Defaults: fetch.min.bytes=1, fetch.max.bytes=50MiB, fetch.max.wait.ms=500, max.partition.fetch.bytes=1MiB.

CompletedFetch decodes batches via a streaming decompressing iterator; under read_committed it drains a firstOffset-ordered abortedTransactions priority queue, tracks abortedProducerIds, removes IDs on abort-marker control batches, and skips entire aborted batches by advancing nextFetchOffset; control records are never delivered (CompletedFetch.java:187,257). FetchCollector caps delivery at max.poll.records (default 500) without changing the wire fetch.

FetchBuffer is a ConcurrentLinkedQueue<CompletedFetch> guarded by a ReentrantLock+Condition, with at most one CompletedFetch per partition; the background thread produces and the application thread consumes/awaits (FetchBuffer.java:50). FetchCollector advances the position only when the response's nextFetchOffset matches the current position, discarding stale responses.

Offset management: enable.auto.commit defaults true with auto.commit.interval.ms=5000; CommitRequestManager.AutoCommitState gates interval commits behind a Timer and hasInflightCommit flag (CommitRequestManager.java:277,1497). commitSync blocks; commitAsync enqueues an AsyncCommitEvent and runs callbacks later on the app thread. With no group.id the CommitRequestManager is absent and commits/committed throw InvalidGroupIdException.

OffsetsRequestManager.updateFetchPositions first validates AWAIT_VALIDATION partitions (OffsetsForLeaderEpoch, log-truncation detection), then for initializing partitions seeds positions from committed offsets (OffsetFetch) and falls back to ListOffsets per auto.offset.reset (default 'latest'); auto.offset.reset=none yields NoOffsetForPartitionException (OffsetsRequestManager.java:235; ConsumerConfig.java:546).

The KIP-848 member state machine (MemberState.java) has states UNSUBSCRIBED, JOINING, RECONCILING, ACKNOWLEDGING, STABLE, FENCED, PREPARE_LEAVING, LEAVING, FATAL, STALE with explicit valid-transition tables; reconciliation order is markPendingRevocation → auto-commit → onPartitionsRevoked → onPartitionsAssigned, with revocations/callbacks deferred to the application thread via background events (AbstractMembershipManager.java:875,993).

Liveness uses a pollTimer initialized to max.poll.interval.ms (default 300000); on expiry the member sends a leave heartbeat and goes STALE, rejoining on the next poll. resetPollTimer runs on every user poll, and maximumTimeToWait() returns at most pollTimer.remainingMs()/2 so the timer cannot silently expire while the app blocks (AbstractHeartbeatRequestManager.java:119,171,262). session.timeout.ms=45000 / heartbeat.interval.ms=3000 apply to the classic protocol.

position (in-memory next-offset, advanced by every fetch, read via position()) is distinct from committed (durable coordinator offset and restart recovery point, read via committed() through a FetchCommittedOffsetsEvent); delivery semantics (at-most-once vs at-least-once) are determined by when the application commits relative to processing.

title: Security: Authentication & Authorization
abstract: A source-grounded reference on Kafka 4.4 (KRaft) wire security and access control. It covers the listener security protocols and the pluggable channel stack (ChannelBuilders → Ssl/SaslChannelBuilder → KafkaChannel with SslTransportLayer and/or a SASL Authenticator), the SASL state machines and the PLAIN/SCRAM/GSSAPI/OAUTHBEARER mechanisms, principal building, and the StandardAuthorizer whose ACLs are replicated as AccessControlEntryRecords in the metadata log and evaluated with deny-wins precedence on every request.
sections: Where it lives in the code
TLS: SslTransportLayer, SslFactory and mutual TLS
keyFacts: Listeners select one of four SecurityProtocols with permanent ids: PLAINTEXT(0), SSL(1), SASL_PLAINTEXT(2), SASL_SSL(3) (clients/.../common/security/auth/SecurityProtocol.java:28); ChannelBuilders.create() dispatches to PlaintextChannelBuilder, SslChannelBuilder, or SaslChannelBuilder (ChannelBuilders.java:109).

On a SASL_SSL server listener, TLS client-cert authentication is forced to NONE unless a listener-prefixed ssl.client.auth is set, because SASL is the identity source (ChannelBuilders.java:152). ssl.client.auth (enum REQUIRED/REQUESTED/NONE) defaults to NONE (BrokerSecurityConfigs.java:86).

The default principal for PLAINTEXT is KafkaPrincipal.ANONYMOUS; for SSL it is the peer certificate X.500 subject mapped via ssl.principal.mapping.rules; for SASL it is User:<authorizationId> (GSSAPI is Kerberos-short-named) — DefaultKafkaPrincipalBuilder.java:68. A KafkaPrincipal.toString() is 'User:name', the exact string used in ACLs and super.users.

SaslServerAuthenticator is a state machine (INITIAL_REQUEST → HANDSHAKE_OR_VERSIONS_REQUEST → AUTHENTICATE → COMPLETE/FAILED, plus REAUTH_PROCESS_HANDSHAKE) driven non-blockingly by the network thread; SASL frames are 4-byte big-endian length + opaque payload (SaslServerAuthenticator.java:108,245). SaslHandshake is apiKey 17, SaslAuthenticate apiKey 36; handshake v1+ wraps tokens in Kafka SaslAuthenticate requests.

SCRAM (RFC 5802) has SCRAM-SHA-256 (type 1) and SCRAM-SHA-512 (type 2), both with 4096..16384 iterations (ScramMechanism.java:32). The stored credential is {salt, storedKey, serverKey, iterations}; storedKey=H(HMAC(saltedPassword,'Client Key')), serverKey=HMAC(saltedPassword,'Server Key') (ScramFormatter.java:93-146). The password is never stored or transmitted; the server verifies the client proof in constant time via MessageDigest.isEqual (ScramSaslServer.java:232).

In KRaft, SCRAM credentials are metadata: ScramControlManager converts a client saltedPassword to keys and emits a UserScramCredentialRecord {Name, Mechanism int8, Salt, StoredKey, ServerKey, Iterations int32} (UserScramCredentialRecord.json; ScramControlManager.java:260). ScramPublisher applies the replicated delta to the CredentialProvider/CredentialCache read by ScramServerCallbackHandler at auth time (ScramPublisher.java:51).

Delegation tokens (KIP-48) are SCRAM-backed: with the SCRAM extension tokenauth=true, ScramSaslServer uses a DelegationTokenCredentialCallback, sets the authorization id to the token owner, and the resulting KafkaPrincipal is flagged tokenAuthenticated(true) (ScramSaslServer.java:112; SaslServerAuthenticator.java:313).

connections.max.reauth.ms (KIP-368, default 0=disabled, BrokerSecurityConfigs.java:109) makes the server compute a session lifetime min(credentialExpiration-now, maxReauth) and return it in SaslAuthenticate.SessionLifetimeMs; re-authentication cannot change the SASL mechanism or principal (SaslServerAuthenticator.java:642,656,667).

StandardAuthorizer (KIP-801) stores ACLs in __cluster_metadata as AccessControlEntryRecords (apiKey 18) with {Id uuid, ResourceType, ResourceName, PatternType, Principal, Host, Operation, PermissionType}; removals use RemoveAccessControlEntryRecord by UUID (AccessControlEntryRecord.json; AclControlManager.java:135). It holds an immutable StandardAuthorizerData behind one volatile field for lock-free reads (StandardAuthorizer.java:72).

AclPublisher applies ACL deltas in commit order (LinkedHashMap iteration) and loads snapshots atomically via loadSnapshot, so a later ALLOW is never transiently visible before an earlier DENY; the first load calls completeInitialLoad(), and until then non-super-users get AuthorizerNotReadyException (AclPublisher.java:80; StandardAuthorizer.java:91; StandardAuthorizerData.java:239).

Authorization precedence is super-user > DENY > ALLOW > default: super.users bypass even DENY, the first matching DENY short-circuits to DENIED, otherwise a matching ALLOW yields ALLOWED, else allow.everyone.if.no.acl.found (default false → DENIED) applies (StandardAuthorizerData.java:226-249,424,584). The authorize() fast path requires a LITERAL action pattern.

ACLs are indexed in an ImmutableNavigableSet sorted by resourceType then REVERSE resource name (StandardAcl.compareTo, StandardAcl.java:83); this makes prefix-matching ACLs contiguous so checkSection walks a bounded region and re-seeks at divergence points, giving sub-linear evaluation (StandardAuthorizerData.java:380). For ALLOW ACLs only, READ/WRITE/DELETE/ALTER imply DESCRIBE and ALTER_CONFIGS implies DESCRIBE_CONFIGS; this implication does not apply to DENY (StandardAuthorizerData.java:436,502).

TLS is the JDK SSLEngine wrapped by SslTransportLayer, handshaked non-blockingly via NEED_WRAP/NEED_UNWRAP/NEED_TASK (SslTransportLayer.java:335); SslFactory hot-reloads keystores but forbids adding/removing a keystore and, by default, DN/SAN changes (ssl.allow.dn.changes/ssl.allow.san.changes default false), validating inter-broker compatibility with an in-memory handshake (SslFactory.java:125,313,402).

OAUTHBEARER carries a JWT; the production BrokerJwtValidator uses a jose4j JwtConsumer that resolves the signing key, requires an expiration time, and optionally enforces expected audience/issuer and clock skew, deriving the principal from the configured sub claim (BrokerJwtValidator.java:79). The default server callback handler is the unsecured validator (test-only).

On authentication failure the server returns a generic 'invalid credentials' message and never echoes SaslException text to avoid leaking user existence (SaslServerAuthenticator.java:487); oversized pre-auth frames beyond sasl.server.max.receive.size (default 524288) are rejected as SaslAuthenticationException (BrokerSecurityConfigs.java:117; SaslServerAuthenticator.java:265).

The broker reaches the authorizer through AuthHelper (core/.../server/AuthHelper.scala:42), which builds literal-pattern Actions; with no authorizer configured every check short-circuits to allowed. AclEntry.supportedOperations defines valid operations per resource type, e.g. TOPIC supports READ/WRITE/CREATE/DESCRIBE/DELETE/ALTER/DESCRIBE_CONFIGS/ALTER_CONFIGS and USER supports CREATE_TOKENS/DESCRIBE_TOKENS (AclEntry.java:53).

title: Quotas, Throttling & Client Metrics
abstract: A source-grounded reference for Kafka 4.4's overload-protection and client-telemetry subsystems. It covers the unified Sensor+Rate/TokenBucket+Quota primitive behind every client quota (produce/fetch byte-rate, request CPU %, controller-mutation, replication and alter-log-dirs throttles), the entity-resolution precedence, the throttle-delay math, the mute-channel/delay-queue/reaper enforcement mechanism, ConnectionQuotas (count + creation rate), how quota limits flow from ClientQuotaRecord metadata through ClientQuotaMetadataManager, and the KIP-714 ClientMetricsManager telemetry path (GetTelemetrySubscriptions/PushTelemetry, subscriptions, push-interval gating, OTLP export).
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: Kafka 4.4 defines nine QuotaTypes in server-common/.../quota/QuotaType.java:19 (FETCH, PRODUCE, REQUEST, CONTROLLER_MUTATION, LEADER_REPLICATION, FOLLOWER_REPLICATION, ALTER_LOG_DIRS_REPLICATION, RLM_COPY, RLM_FETCH); the first four map to ClientQuotaType and are managed by ClientQuotaManager subclasses.

All client quotas share one primitive: a common.metrics.Sensor holding a sampled Rate (WindowedSum) or a TokenBucket stat, with the limit stored in MetricConfig.quota() and enforced by Sensor.checkQuotas (Sensor.java:253), which throws QuotaViolationException when value crosses the bound (or tokens<0 for a TokenBucket).

Throttle delay for rate quotas is X = (O-T)/T * W where O=observed rate, T=target/bound, W=current window size (QuotaUtils.throttleTime, server-common/.../quota/QuotaUtils.java:41). The request quota and connection-rate caps this at one window (quota.window.size.seconds, default 1s).

Quota entity resolution follows an 8-level precedence (user+client-id, user+default-client, user, default-user+client, default-user+default-client, default-user, client-id, default-client) implemented in DefaultQuotaCallback.findQuota (ClientQuotaManager.java:747); a quotaTypesEnabled bitmask gives an O(1) fast path when only one level is configured.

Throttling holds the connection: ClientQuotaManager.throttle enqueues a ThrottledChannel (a java.util.concurrent.Delayed ordered by endTimeNanos) on a DelayQueue and calls startThrottling (mute); a per-quota-type daemon thread named <prefix>ThrottledChannelReaper-<QuotaType> polls the queue and calls endThrottling (unmute) when the delay expires (ClientQuotaManager.java:276-300). The user response carrying throttle_time_ms is sent immediately so clients back off.

Controller-mutation quota (KIP-599) uses a TokenBucket with burst = samples * windowMs * quota; throttle = round(-tokens/quota*1000) ms. ControllerApis wires StrictControllerMutationQuota (rejects with THROTTLING_QUOTA_EXCEEDED) for CreateTopics v5+, CreatePartitions v6+, DeleteTopics v3+, and PermissiveControllerMutationQuota below those versions (ControllerMutationQuotaManager.java:169, ControllerApis.scala:200/364/801).

The request quota measures CPU time, not bytes: ClientRequestQuotaManager records request-handler thread time as a percentage (NANOS_TO_PERCENTAGE_PER_SECOND=100/1e9) plus network-thread send time via a callback, and records internal/cluster-action traffic against a separate never-expiring 'exempt-Request' sensor (ClientRequestQuotaManager.java:78-96).

Default quota config values (server-common/.../config/QuotaConfig.java): quota.window.num=11 (10 whole + 1 current), quota.window.size.seconds=1, client.quota.callback.class=null; entity overrides producer_byte_rate/consumer_byte_rate default Long.MAX_VALUE, request_percentage/controller_mutation_rate default Integer.MAX_VALUE.

ConnectionQuotas (SocketServer.scala:1276) enforces connection COUNT (max.connections, max.connections.per.ip, both default Integer.MAX_VALUE) and connection CREATION RATE (max.connection.creation.rate broker/listener and per-IP connection_creation_rate) inside the acceptor under counts.synchronized; the inter-broker listener is 'protected' and bypasses broker-wide limits when multiple listeners exist.

Quota limits are stored as ClientQuotaRecord entries in the KRaft metadata log (ClientQuotaControlManager); ClientQuotaMetadataManager.accept applies a ClientQuotasDelta, routing user/client keys to manager.updateQuota and IP keys to connectionQuotas.updateIpConnectionRateQuota (ClientQuotaMetadataManager.scala:57). Changing a limit re-sets MetricConfig without resetting accumulated usage.

KIP-714 client telemetry is handled by ClientMetricsManager (server/.../ClientMetricsManager.java) via two RPCs: GetTelemetrySubscriptions (assigns a UUID clientInstanceId, returns resolved metric set, subscriptionId, pushIntervalMs, deltaTemporality=true) and PushTelemetry (validates and exports OTLP bytes via clientTelemetryExporterPlugin.exportMetrics). telemetry.max.bytes defaults to 1 MiB (MetricConfigs.java:66).

Telemetry subscriptions are broker config (metrics, interval.ms default 300000ms clamped 100ms-1h, match patterns); the subscriptionId = CRC32C(metrics.toString()+pushIntervalMs) XOR clientInstanceId.hashCode() (ClientMetricsManager.java:368). Each client is gated to one push per pushIntervalMs by ClientMetricsInstance.maybeUpdatePushRequestTimestamp; too-early pushes get ThrottlingQuotaExceededException. Instances live in a 16384-entry LRU cache with a client-metrics-reaper expiration thread.

title: Kafka Streams Architecture
abstract: A reference chapter on Apache Kafka Streams: the client-side stream-processing library built on the consumer/producer clients. It traces the full path from DSL/Processor-API topology construction (sub-topologies, repartition and changelog topics) through the task model (StreamTask/StandbyTask owning one partition-group), the StreamThread poll/process/punctuate/commit loop, task assignment (co-partitioning, sticky placement, KIP-441 warmup/probing and the KIP-1071 broker-driven protocol), state stores and the record cache, changelog-based restoration on a dedicated state-updater thread, and exactly-once-v2 via per-thread producer transactions. Every claim is grounded in the 4.4.0-SNAPSHOT source.
sections: Where it lives in the code
From DSL/Processor API to a topology
State restoration: the changelog reader & state updater
keyFacts: A Kafka Streams application is a DAG of ProcessorNodes built by InternalTopologyBuilder; the DSL (KStreamImpl/KTableImpl) is a thin compiler that translates a logical graph in InternalStreamsBuilder.buildAndOptimizeTopology() into the same Processor-API addSource/addProcessor/addSink calls (InternalStreamsBuilder.java:304).

The topology is split into sub-topologies (node groups) by a union-find that breaks the graph at repartition-topic boundaries (InternalTopologyBuilder.makeNodeGroups, :937); each node group becomes one entry in subtopologyToTopicsInfo with sourceTopics/sinkTopics/repartitionSourceTopics/stateChangelogTopics (TopicsInfo, :1209).

A task is the unit of parallelism: TaskId = (subtopology, partition[, topologyName]) (processor/TaskId.java:43); a StreamTask owns one partition from each input topic of its sub-topology (a partition group), while a StandbyTask is a passive hot replica that only replays the changelog (StandbyTask.java:46).

Each KafkaStreams instance runs num.stream.threads StreamThreads (default 1), each with its own main consumer, group-less restore consumer, shared admin client, exactly one StreamsProducer (ActiveTaskCreator.java:95), a per-thread ThreadCache, and its own DefaultStateUpdater restoration thread (StreamThread.create, :399).

The StreamThread loop runOnceWithoutProcessingThreads does poll -> add records to task queues -> checkStateUpdater -> process up to numIterations records/task -> punctuate -> commit (StreamThread.java:1198); numIterations adapts and halves when near max.poll.interval.ms or after a commit/punctuate to avoid being kicked from the group (:1298).

A StreamTask is only processable when every input partition queue has data (or max.task.idle.ms elapsed) and there is no pending TX commit; partitionGroup.nextRecord picks the smallest-timestamp record across partitions, advancing monotonic stream-time (StreamTask.isProcessable :716, doProcess :868).

State restoration runs off the StreamThread on a dedicated StateUpdaterThread (DefaultStateUpdater.java:82,:193) which calls StoreChangelogReader.restore; changelogs move REGISTERED->RESTORING->COMPLETED, an active changelog completes when its store offset reaches the end offset, and an InvalidOffsetException becomes TaskCorruptedException to rebuild from scratch (StoreChangelogReader.java:439,:499,:692).

The classic StreamsPartitionAssignor encodes per-task offset sums + processId in SubscriptionInfo (:281) and uses the default HighAvailabilityTaskAssignor (KIP-441): active tasks go to instances caught up within acceptable.recovery.lag (default 10000), otherwise a warmup standby is created (capped by max.warmup.replicas, default 2) and a probing rebalance every probing.rebalance.interval.ms (default 600000) moves the active task once warm.

Within an instance assignTasksToThreads stickily distributes tasks to threads, giving each thread back its previously-owned stateful tasks ordered by lag (prevTasksByLag) so open RocksDB stores are reused (StreamsPartitionAssignor.java:1293).

With group.protocol=streams (KIP-1071, Early Access; default is classic), assignment, internal-topic creation and warmup are driven by the broker group coordinator via the StreamsGroupHeartbeat RPC and broker-side StreamsGroup, and the client cannot trigger probing/enforced rebalances (StreamThread.java:556,:981,:1531).

State stores are a decorator stack Metered -> Caching -> ChangeLogging -> RocksDBStore/InMemory; the per-thread byte-bounded write-back ThreadCache (statestore.cache.max.bytes, default 10 MiB) flushes dirty entries on eviction/commit, suppressing intermediate results — setting it to 0 emits every update (ThreadCache.java:39,:294; StreamsConfig.java:956).

exactly_once_v2 uses one transactional StreamsProducer per thread (transactional.id = applicationId-processId-threadIdx) that atomically commits output records, changelog records and input offsets via sendOffsetsToTransaction+commitTransaction for ALL the thread's active tasks; ProducerFenced/InvalidProducerEpoch is mapped to TaskMigratedException (TaskExecutor.java:173; StreamsProducer.java:185,:242; ActiveTaskCreator.java:104). Enabling EOS lowers default commit.interval.ms from 30000 to 100 ms.

Committed input offsets carry encoded TopicPartitionMetadata holding the partition stream-time and serialized ProcessorMetadata so a restarted task restores its time/state (StreamTask.findOffsetAndMetadata :465,:1075); ProcessorStateManager.changelogOffsets returns offset+1 per changelog and drives the on-disk checkpoint and assignor lag (ProcessorStateManager.java:446).

Interactive Queries v2: KafkaStreams.query(StateQueryRequest) dispatches typed queries (KeyQuery/RangeQuery/Window queries) to local store partitions returning StateQueryResult, and StreamsMetadataState.queryMetadataForKey routes a key to the owning active host plus standby hosts for application-level forwarding (query/StateQueryRequest.java).

title: Kafka Connect Architecture
abstract: A reference chapter on Apache Kafka Connect derived directly from the connect/ source tree. It covers the plugin SPI (SourceConnector/SinkConnector + SourceTask/SinkTask, Converters, SMTs/Predicates), the Worker container and per-task execution loops (WorkerSourceTask/WorkerSinkTask), distributed coordination via the DistributedHerder and IncrementalCooperativeAssignor (KIP-415), the three internal compacted topics (config/offsets/status), exactly-once source (KIP-618), and MirrorMaker 2. Every concrete claim is grounded with file:line citations.
sections: Where it lives in the code
keyFacts: A Kafka Connect connector plans work and runs as exactly one instance; it splits a job into at most tasks.max task configs via Connector.taskConfigs(int) (default tasks.max=1), while tasks (SourceTask/SinkTask) perform the actual IO. tasks.max.enforce defaults to true, failing connectors that emit too many tasks (connect/runtime/.../runtime/Worker.java:412, ConnectorConfig.java:137).

Each running task executes on its own thread named task-thread-<connector>-<index>, submitted to the Worker's Executors.newCachedThreadPool(); WorkerTask is the Runnable base managing pause/resume/stop via the task's monitor and volatile flags (Worker.java:176, WorkerTask.java:67/333).

Source tasks own a KafkaProducer; AbstractWorkerSourceTask does poll -> SMT chain -> key/value/header Converter -> producer.send, and tracks in-flight records in SubmittedRecords so offsets flush only behind the longest fully-acknowledged prefix (AbstractWorkerSourceTask.java:408, WorkerSourceTask.java:141/337).

Source offsets are flushed asynchronously by a single-thread SourceTaskOffsetCommitter every offset.flush.interval.ms (default 60000) within an offset.flush.timeout.ms budget (default 5000), writing the offsets topic via OffsetStorageWriter (SourceTaskOffsetCommitter.java:77, WorkerConfig.java:116/121).

Sink tasks join an ordinary Kafka consumer group, subscribe to topics/topics.regex, and commit to __consumer_offsets; WorkerSinkTask calls task.preCommit() for safe offsets, uses a commitSeqno to ignore out-of-order async commit callbacks, and drives task.open()/close() from a HandleRebalance ConsumerRebalanceListener (WorkerSinkTask.java:324/288/732).

Distributed Connect persists durable state in three compacted Kafka topics: config.storage.topic (enforced single partition, a totally-ordered WAL), offset.storage.topic (default 25 partitions, source offsets), and status.storage.topic (default 5 partitions) (KafkaConfigBackingStore.java:390, DistributedConfig.java:138/168).

The config topic uses string keys with prefixes connector-, task-, commit-, target-state-, tasks-fencing-, session-key, restart-connector-; task configs are buffered and applied atomically only when a commit-<connector> marker with a complete task-id set is read, preventing half-written reconfigurations even under compaction (KafkaConfigBackingStore.java:197/1107).

The DistributedHerder runs almost all logic on a single tick thread that drives group membership, reconciles config snapshots, and serves REST requests (enqueued as DistributedHerderRequest); heavy connector/task start-stop is offloaded to an 8-thread StartAndStopExecutor (DistributedHerder.java:145/333).

The assignor-elected leader is the sole writer of connector configs, session keys and task-count records to the config topic; non-leaders forward config-change REST requests to the leader URL, and internal worker REST calls are HMAC-signed with a rotating session key under protocol V2 'sessioned' (KIP-507) (DistributedHerder.java:2677/354).

The default IncrementalCooperativeAssignor (KIP-415) computes a minimal assignment delta over set-algebra snapshots (configured/active/deleted/duplicated/lost/created) so unchanged tasks are never interrupted, unlike the stop-the-world EagerAssignor (IncrementalCooperativeAssignor.java:58/197).

When a worker leaves, its lost tasks are not reassigned immediately; reassignment is deferred up to scheduled.rebalance.max.delay.ms (default 300000) and the departed worker is remembered as a candidate so a rejoining worker reclaims its own work (IncrementalCooperativeAssignor.java:439/470, DistributedConfig.java:193).

The Connect group protocol carries a worker subscription (Url + furthest ConfigOffset) and a leader-computed assignment (Leader, LeaderUrl, ConfigOffset, per-connector task list); task id -1 (CONNECTOR_TASK) signals running the Connector instance itself; V1/V2 add Revoked lists and a scheduled-rebalance Delay (ConnectProtocol.java:55/114, IncrementalCooperativeConnectProtocol.java:59).

Exactly-once source (KIP-618, since Kafka 3.3) is enabled by exactly.once.source.support (default disabled); ExactlyOnceWorkerSourceTask writes records and source offsets in one producer transaction with boundaries per transaction.boundary (POLL default / INTERVAL / CONNECTOR), and the worker fences zombie producers using tasks-fencing task-count records (DistributedConfig.java:251, ExactlyOnceWorkerSourceTask.java:124, Worker.java:764).

Status changes propagate via KafkaStatusBackingStore and become authoritative only after round-tripping the status topic; putSafe() plus per-entry sequence numbers shrink the stale-overwrite window, and a DESTROYED status serializes to a null compaction tombstone (KafkaStatusBackingStore.java:70/293).

Converters are independent for key, value and header (JsonConverter, StringConverter, etc.) implementing fromConnectData/toConnectData; a TransformationStage applies its Transformation only when negate XOR predicate.test(record) holds, and stages compose into a TransformationChain (Converter.java:73, TransformationStage.java:90).

MirrorMaker 2 is built as Connect source connectors in connect/mirror: MirrorSourceConnector replicates topic data/configs/ACLs and round-robins source topic-partitions across min(maxTasks, partitionCount) tasks, MirrorHeartbeatConnector emits heartbeat SourceRecords, and MirrorCheckpointConnector translates consumer-group offsets across clusters (MirrorSourceConnector.java:202, MirrorHeartbeatTask.java:64, MirrorCheckpointConnector.java:132).
